items.prior_work: Adam (Kingma & Ba, 2015) is the standard adaptive optimizer for transformer pretraining, maintaining per-parameter first- and second-moment estimates of the gradient and normalizing each update by the square root of the second moment. Because these moment estimates are unreliable in the first steps, training is typically stabilized with a learning-rate warmup: the schedule popularized by Vaswani et al. (2017) ramps the learning rate linearly from zero to a peak over a fixed number of steps, then decays it (originally as an inverse square root of the step count, later often cosine). Warmup length and peak learning rate are set by hand and treated as standard, transferable hyperparameters.
items.base: Replace the linear warmup ramp with a polynomial ramp whose exponent p is treated as a tunable hyperparameter, so the learning rate rises as (t/T_w)^p over a warmup window of length T_w. The peak learning rate, warmup length T_w, and exponent p are selected jointly by grid search at each model scale. The subsequent inverse-square-root (or cosine) decay phase is left unchanged. The method is evaluated by comparing final validation perplexity and training stability against the linear-warmup baseline across several model sizes.
items.sig: Warmup is among the least understood yet most consequential phases of large-model pretraining, and its geometry has largely been fixed by default to a single linear ramp. This work reframes the warmup trajectory itself as a continuous design space, parameterizing the ascent as (t/T_w)^p and elevating the curvature exponent p to a first-class optimization target alongside the peak learning rate and the warmup length T_w, jointly selected at each model scale. Because the linear ramp is recovered exactly at p=1, the formulation embeds current practice as a single interior point of a far richer family, letting the optimizer's most volatile interval be shaped rather than merely bounded. The inverse-square-root or cosine decay phase is deliberately held fixed, isolating the contribution of warmup curvature alone. We assess the consequences for both final validation perplexity and training stability across a range of model sizes, treating the exponent as a scale-dependent control whose optimum may itself expose structure in how adaptive optimizers stabilize early training.
items.pad: Borrowing the language of driven non-equilibrium systems, treat the warmup phase as a quench protocol that ramps a control field (the learning rate) from a quiescent zero state toward its operating point. Rather than the conventional constant-slope (linear) drive, impose a power-law ramp in which the learning rate follows (t/T_w)^p across a warmup window of duration T_w, so that p plays the role of a tunable scaling exponent governing how sharply the system approaches its steady drive — a knob directly analogous to the critical exponent controlling a power-law approach to a critical point in the theory of phase transitions. The exponent p, the window T_w, and the peak (operating) learning rate are co-optimized by a grid sweep at each model scale, treated as the three coordinates parameterizing the quench. The post-warmup relaxation stage (inverse-square-root or cosine decay) is left as-is. The protocol is assessed by comparing steady-state validation perplexity and the transient stability of the training trajectory against the linear-drive baseline across several model sizes.

items.prior_work: AdamW (Loshchilov & Hutter, 2019) decouples weight decay from the adaptive gradient update, applying the decay directly to the weights rather than adding it to the loss gradient before the moment estimates are computed. In the standard implementation the decay term is still multiplied by the current learning rate, so a learning-rate schedule (e.g., cosine or warmup-decay) also scales the effective per-step weight decay. Practitioners typically tune the decay coefficient and the learning-rate schedule independently, treating decoupled weight decay as a fixed regularization strength. The interaction between the two — that the schedule modulates the realized regularization — is acknowledged but usually left implicit.
items.base: Introduce a separate schedule for the decoupled weight-decay coefficient so that the realized per-step regularization is controlled independently of the learning-rate schedule. The decay term is divided by the current learning rate, normalized against a reference value, so that a cosine or warmup-decay learning-rate schedule no longer scales the effective weight decay; the coefficient may then follow its own tuned schedule. The moment estimates, adaptive step, and optimizer state are unchanged — only the weight-decay term's dependence on the learning rate is removed. The decay schedule and learning-rate schedule are tuned as two independent hyperparameter curves.
items.sig: Weight decay and the learning-rate schedule are almost universally entangled: in decoupled optimizers the realized per-step regularization still rides on the learning-rate curve, so a cosine or warmup-decay schedule silently modulates how strongly the model is regularized at every phase of training. This work severs that coupling. By dividing the decay term by the current learning rate and normalizing against a reference value, the effective regularization is rendered invariant to the learning-rate schedule, freeing the weight-decay coefficient to follow its own independently tuned trajectory. The moment estimates, adaptive step, and optimizer state are left entirely intact — the intervention is surgical, touching only the learning-rate dependence of the decay term. The effect is to recast a single fused hyperparameter schedule as two orthogonal control curves, opening the regularization schedule as a design axis that has until now been an uncontrolled byproduct of learning-rate tuning.
items.pad: Cast the optimizer as a driven dissipative dynamical system in which the adaptive update supplies the drive and weight decay acts as a linear damping (dissipation) term. In the usual coupling this damping is modulated by the same time-varying gain as the drive — the learning rate — so any annealing of the gain inadvertently detunes the realized dissipation. Introduce a gain-normalization step in the spirit of feedback linearization from control theory: divide the decay term by the instantaneous learning rate and normalize it against a fixed reference gain, so that a cosine or warmup-decay learning-rate trajectory no longer scales the effective damping. Freed from this coupling, the damping coefficient can then track its own homeostatic set-point schedule. The moment estimates, adaptive step, and optimizer state — the internal drive dynamics — remain untouched; only the learning-rate dependence of the dissipation term is removed. The damping (weight-decay) schedule and the gain (learning-rate) schedule are subsequently tuned as two independent control curves.

items.prior_work: Adaptive moment methods such as Adam and AdamW maintain two per-parameter state buffers — a first-moment (momentum) estimate and a second-moment (variance) estimate — which together roughly double the memory required beyond the model weights. Lion (Chen et al., 2023), discovered through symbolic program search over optimizer update rules, instead tracks only a single momentum buffer and applies the sign of an interpolation between the momentum and the current gradient, producing a uniform-magnitude update per coordinate. Because it stores one state buffer rather than two, Lion approximately halves optimizer-state memory while matching or exceeding Adam-family performance on several large-scale training tasks. Its update magnitude is decoupled from gradient scale, which typically calls for a smaller learning rate and larger weight decay than Adam.
items.base: Extend Lion by storing its single momentum buffer in a low-precision, block-wise quantized format (for example 8-bit per element with per-block scaling factors) rather than 16- or 32-bit floating point. The momentum is dequantized to compute the sign-momentum update and requantized after each step, so the sign-based update rule is unchanged and only the storage representation of the state differs. This reduces Lion's remaining optimizer-state memory by roughly half, and because the update depends only on the sign of the interpolated momentum, moderate quantization error on the buffer is expected to have limited effect on the update direction. Evaluation compares training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and against 8-bit Adam.
items.sig: Lion already halves optimizer-state memory relative to adaptive moment methods by carrying a single momentum buffer, which makes that lone buffer the natural frontier for further compression. This work presses against that frontier by storing the momentum in a low-precision, block-wise quantized format — for example 8-bit per element with per-block scaling factors — dequantizing only to form the sign-momentum update and requantizing after each step. A structural property of the algorithm makes this especially well-motivated: because the update depends solely on the sign of the interpolated momentum, the buffer's numerical precision is a far weaker determinant of the update direction than in value-sensitive optimizers, so moderate quantization error is absorbed rather than propagated. The sign-based update rule is preserved exactly; only the storage representation of the state changes, cutting Lion's residual optimizer-state memory by roughly half. We situate the method between full-precision Lion and 8-bit Adam, comparing training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint to chart where sign-based updates and aggressive state quantization compound.
items.pad: View Lion's momentum buffer through the lens of lossy source coding: it is a stored signal whose sole functional readout is the polarity of an interpolation, so under a rate-distortion analysis most of its bit-precision is redundant channel capacity. Encode the buffer with a block-wise scalar quantizer — for example 8-bit codewords sharing a per-block gain (scaling) factor — in place of a 16- or 32-bit floating-point representation. On each step the codeword is decoded back to a real value to form the sign-momentum update and re-encoded afterward, so the update rule (the actual computation) is bit-for-bit identical and only the channel representation of the state changes. This roughly halves Lion's residual optimizer-state memory. Because the update extracts only the sign — the polarity bit — of the interpolated momentum, quantization noise injected into the lower-order bits is expected to leave the decision boundary, and hence the update direction, largely intact, much as a threshold/sign code is robust to sub-threshold channel perturbations. Evaluation compares training curves, final accuracy, and memory footprint against full-precision Lion and against 8-bit Adam.

items.prior_work: Contextual sentence embeddings from models such as BERT are anisotropic: their vectors occupy a narrow cone, so cosine similarities are compressed and correlate poorly with human semantic-similarity judgments. BERT-whitening (Su et al., 2021) addresses this post hoc by estimating the corpus mean and covariance of embeddings and applying a linear transformation that centers them and sets the covariance to identity, optionally reducing dimensionality. SimCSE (Gao et al., 2021) instead improves the geometry during training through a contrastive objective, using dropout-perturbed pairs (unsupervised) or NLI pairs (supervised), which flattens the singular-value spectrum. Both approaches treat increased isotropy of the overall embedding distribution as the target for better semantic-similarity performance.
items.base: Apply the whitening transformation to embeddings produced by a contrastively trained encoder rather than a plain masked-language-model encoder, and replace the empirical covariance with a shrinkage-regularized estimate that interpolates between the sample covariance and a scaled identity. The shrinkage coefficient is selected on held-out data to stabilize the whitening matrix when the corpus is small or the dimension is large. The centering vector and whitening matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear post-processing step, with optional dimensionality reduction, and performance is measured on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks against whitening that uses the unregularized covariance.
items.sig: Whitening has become a standard remedy for anisotropy in sentence embeddings, yet it has been applied almost exclusively to masked-language-model encoders and rests on a fragile empirical covariance estimate. This work relocates the technique to the representations of a contrastively trained encoder — a regime where the geometry of the embedding space is qualitatively different — and hardens its statistical core by replacing the raw sample covariance with a shrinkage-regularized estimator that interpolates between the empirical covariance and a scaled identity. The shrinkage coefficient, selected on held-out data, directly targets the small-corpus, high-dimension regime in which the whitening matrix is most ill-conditioned and unregularized whitening is least reliable. Centering vector and whitening matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear post-processing step with optional dimensionality reduction, keeping the method inference-cheap and architecture-agnostic. By benchmarking against unregularized whitening on standard semantic-similarity tasks, the study isolates the compounded benefit of pairing contrastive geometry with a robust covariance estimate — two ingredients the field has so far treated in isolation.
items.pad: Frame the geometry problem as one of efficient sensory coding: an anisotropic embedding cloud is a redundant, correlated code, and 'sphering' it — the decorrelating whitening long studied for retinal and early-visual representations — is precisely the transform that removes second-order redundancy and maximizes isotropy. Apply this sphering operation to the embeddings of a contrastively trained encoder rather than a plain masked-language-model encoder. Because in the high-dimensional regime, where the embedding dimension is comparable to the sample size, the empirical covariance is an ill-conditioned and spectrally distorted estimator — a failure mode that random-matrix theory makes quantitative — replace it with a shrinkage-regularized estimate that interpolates between the sample covariance and a scaled identity, damping the unreliable small eigenvalues. The shrinkage coefficient is chosen on held-out data to stabilize the sphering operator when the corpus is small or the dimension is large. The centering vector and whitening (sphering) matrix are estimated once over the target corpus and applied as a fixed linear post-processing map, with optional dimensionality reduction, and performance is measured on standard semantic-similarity benchmarks against sphering that uses the unregularized covariance.

items.prior_work: Self-supervised methods learn representations by enforcing invariance between augmented views of an image, but without safeguards the embeddings can collapse. VICReg (Bardes et al., 2022) prevents this with three terms: an invariance loss between views, a variance term that keeps the standard deviation of each embedding dimension above a fixed threshold, and a covariance term that drives off-diagonal covariances toward zero to decorrelate dimensions. Jing et al. (2022) analyzed a related failure, dimensional collapse, in which embedding vectors occupy a lower-dimensional subspace, tracing it to strong augmentation and implicit regularization and showing that the representation's covariance spectrum has many near-zero singular values. Together these works frame anti-collapse regularization around the second-order statistics of the embedding and a fixed, isotropy-oriented target.
items.base: Apply VICReg's variance and covariance regularization beyond the projector output to several intermediate backbone layers. At each selected depth, the per-dimension variance hinge keeps embedding coordinates above a floor and the off-diagonal covariance penalty decorrelates them, using depth-specific weights tuned on a validation split; the invariance term is unchanged. The aim is to limit dimensional collapse that develops inside the encoder rather than only at the final embedding, evaluated by per-layer effective rank and downstream linear-probe accuracy.
items.sig: Dimensional collapse is typically diagnosed and countered only at the final embedding, leaving the interior of the encoder — where representational capacity is actually built up — outside the reach of existing regularizers. This work extends VICReg's variance and covariance criteria inward, applying the per-dimension variance hinge and the off-diagonal decorrelation penalty at several intermediate backbone depths rather than at the projector output alone, with depth-specific weights tuned on a validation split and the invariance term left unchanged. The reframing treats collapse not as a terminal property of the output head but as a phenomenon that accumulates layer by layer, and it instruments that view directly through per-layer effective rank alongside downstream linear-probe accuracy. In doing so it opens the encoder's internal geometry as a controllable target, positioning depth-resolved variance-covariance regularization as a route to preserving representational richness where it originates rather than where it is finally read out.
items.pad: Treat the encoder as a cascade of information-processing stages, each of which — by a data-processing-inequality intuition — can only forward or discard, never create, the effective degrees of freedom it inherits; dimensional collapse is then the premature loss of participation ratio (effective rank) partway down the cascade. To arrest it, apply VICReg's variance and covariance penalties not only at the projector output but at several intermediate backbone stages. At each selected depth a per-coordinate variance hinge holds each dimension's spread above a floor — preserving that stage's spectral entropy — while the off-diagonal covariance penalty decorrelates the coordinates to strip second-order redundancy, with depth-specific weights tuned on a validation split; the invariance term is unchanged. The goal is to counter the collapse of effective dimensionality as it forms inside the encoder rather than only at the terminal embedding, assessed by per-layer effective rank (participation ratio) and downstream linear-probe accuracy.

items.prior_work: Kumar et al. (2022) analyze linear probing (training only the classification head) versus full fine-tuning under distribution shift, and show that fine-tuning can distort pretrained features and yield worse out-of-distribution (OOD) accuracy than linear probing even when in-distribution (ID) accuracy is higher. Using a two-layer linear network, they argue the distortion concentrates in feature directions that the source data does not constrain. They propose LP-FT: first train the linear head to convergence, then fine-tune the whole network. This two-stage recipe reduces distortion and improves both ID and OOD accuracy, and has become a standard baseline for robust transfer.
items.base: During the fine-tuning stage, apply layer-wise learning-rate decay that assigns smaller learning rates to the feature extractor and larger rates to the classification head, bounding how far pretrained features move. Sweep the feature-extractor learning rate and measure in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, mapping the resulting ID–OOD accuracy frontier and comparing it against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune.
items.sig: We treat the tension between preserving pretrained representations and adapting them to a target task as a controllable continuum rather than a binary choice. During the fine-tuning stage, layer-wise learning-rate decay assigns smaller learning rates to the feature extractor and larger rates to the classification head, directly bounding how far pretrained features are permitted to move — a single, interpretable dial governing feature preservation. By sweeping the feature-extractor learning rate and measuring in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, we trace the full ID–OOD accuracy frontier, situating standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune as two points along the spectrum this formulation spans. The resulting frontier offers a principled account of the robustness–plasticity trade-off that governs how pretrained models should be adapted under distribution shift, with direct implications for reliable deployment.
items.pad: Cast adaptation as a slow–fast dynamical system in the spirit of singular-perturbation theory, treating the feature extractor as a slowly relaxing manifold and the classification head as the fast variables that equilibrate on top of it. Concretely, during the fine-tuning stage assign each depth its own relaxation timescale by applying layer-wise learning-rate decay — small learning rates (low mobility) to the feature extractor and larger learning rates to the classification head — so that the pretrained features evolve quasi-adiabatically and their displacement in parameter space stays bounded. Borrowing the language of multi-objective thermodynamics, sweep the feature-extractor learning rate as a control parameter and measure in-distribution and out-of-distribution accuracy across several shift magnitudes and datasets, tracing the resulting ID–OOD accuracy frontier as a Pareto front and comparing it against standard fine-tuning and linear-probe-then-fine-tune.

items.prior_work: BYOL (Grill et al., 2020) and SimSiam (Chen & He, 2021) learn image representations without negative pairs by matching the embeddings of two augmented views of the same image. BYOL uses an online network with a predictor head plus a momentum-averaged target network, while SimSiam removes the momentum encoder and shows that a stop-gradient operation on one branch is what prevents the network from collapsing to a constant output. In both methods, the stop-gradient and the associated branch asymmetry are the central ingredient credited with maintaining stability. Related negative-free methods such as Barlow Twins and VICReg instead prevent collapse with explicit variance and covariance regularization.
items.base: Train the SimSiam-style two-view architecture with gradients flowing symmetrically through both branches (no stop-gradient and no momentum encoder), and prevent collapse by adding a regularization term that pushes the batch-wise feature covariance toward the identity: a hinge penalty maintaining per-dimension variance above a floor plus an off-diagonal covariance penalty. The invariance loss and the covariance regularizer are applied identically to both views. The relative weight of the regularizer is treated as a tunable hyperparameter, and the predictor head is retained as an optional component.
items.sig: This design isolates the mechanism responsible for preventing representational collapse and relocates it from architectural asymmetry into an explicit, statistically interpretable constraint. We train the SimSiam-style two-view architecture with gradients flowing symmetrically through both branches — dispensing with both the stop-gradient and the momentum encoder — and instead forestall collapse by driving the batch-wise feature covariance toward the identity: a hinge penalty holding per-dimension variance above a floor together with an off-diagonal covariance penalty. The invariance loss and the covariance regularizer act identically on both views, giving a symmetric objective whose collapse-avoidance is governed transparently by the regularizer weight, treated as a tunable hyperparameter, with the predictor head retained only as an optional component. By recasting a property previously entrusted to delicate architectural machinery as a direct constraint on second-order feature statistics, this formulation offers a cleaner vantage point on why negative-free methods avoid collapse and what is genuinely essential to their stability.
items.pad: Reframe collapse avoidance as an information-theoretic redundancy-reduction problem in the tradition of efficient-coding theories of sensory neurons: a degenerate encoder is one whose channels carry redundant or vanishing information, so stability ought to follow from maximizing the effective coding capacity of the representation rather than from any branch asymmetry. Accordingly, train the SimSiam-style two-view architecture with gradients flowing symmetrically through both branches (no stop-gradient and no momentum encoder), and prevent collapse by driving the batch-wise feature covariance toward the identity — an isotropic whitening of the code: a hinge penalty that holds each channel's variance above a floor (guarding against informational collapse) plus an off-diagonal covariance penalty that decorrelates the channels to remove redundancy. The invariance term and this decorrelation regularizer are applied identically to both views. The relative weight of the regularizer is treated as a tunable hyperparameter, and the predictor head is retained as an optional component.

items.prior_work: Soft Actor-Critic (Haarnoja et al., 2018) is an off-policy actor-critic method for continuous control that augments the reward with a policy-entropy term, trading off return against exploration. The strength of this term is set by a temperature, or entropy coefficient. To avoid hand-tuning it, SAC formulates a constrained objective in which the policy maximizes return subject to a minimum expected-entropy constraint, and adjusts the temperature online by dual gradient descent to meet a fixed target entropy. The target entropy is set by a heuristic, commonly the negative of the action-space dimensionality.
items.base: Replace the single scalar temperature with a vector of temperature coefficients, one per action dimension. Each coefficient is adjusted independently by dual gradient descent to meet its own per-dimension target entropy, for example a target of -1 per dimension, which recovers the standard aggregate target when summed across dimensions. The policy update uses the per-dimension coefficients to weight the corresponding entropy terms. This lets the temperature adapt separately across action dimensions that differ in scale or difficulty, while leaving the maximum-entropy objective, critic updates, and dual-descent tuning procedure otherwise unchanged.
items.sig: We give the maximum-entropy control framework the resolution to allocate exploration anisotropically across action dimensions — a degree of freedom the single scalar temperature cannot express. The scalar temperature is replaced by a vector of coefficients, one per action dimension, each driven independently by dual gradient descent toward its own per-dimension target entropy (for instance, -1 per dimension), a construction that exactly recovers the standard aggregate target when summed across dimensions and therefore contains the scalar formulation as a special case. The policy update weights each entropy term by its corresponding coefficient, letting the effective temperature track the differing scale and difficulty of individual action dimensions, while the maximum-entropy objective, the critic updates, and the dual-descent tuning procedure remain otherwise intact. The result is a more expressive, finely resolved treatment of exploration in continuous control, of particular consequence as action spaces grow in dimension and heterogeneity.
items.pad: Draw an explicit analogy to multi-thermostat molecular dynamics, in which each degree of freedom is coupled to its own heat bath rather than to a single global temperature, so that equipartition can be enforced mode by mode. In that spirit, replace the single scalar temperature with a vector of temperature coefficients, one per action dimension — one thermostat per degree of freedom. Each coefficient is adjusted independently by dual gradient descent, acting as a feedback thermostat that drives its dimension to its own per-dimension target-entropy setpoint, for example a target of -1 per dimension, which recovers the standard aggregate target when summed across dimensions (the equipartition limit). The policy update uses the per-dimension coefficients to weight the corresponding entropy terms, letting the local temperature adapt separately across action dimensions that differ in scale or difficulty, while leaving the maximum-entropy objective, critic updates, and dual-descent tuning procedure otherwise unchanged.

items.prior_work: Off-policy value learning with the max operator systematically overestimates action values, because maximizing over noisy Q-estimates induces a positive bias (Thrun & Schwartz; van Hasselt). Double DQN (van Hasselt et al., 2016) mitigates this by decoupling action selection from action evaluation, using the online network to choose the greedy action and the target network to evaluate it. TD3 (Fujimoto et al., 2018) introduced clipped double-Q learning for continuous-control actor-critic methods, maintaining two critics and taking the minimum of their target estimates, alongside target policy smoothing and delayed policy updates. These fixed, structural corrections are the consensus baselines for controlling overestimation in off-policy value learning.
items.base: Extend TD3's clipped double-Q learning by replacing the minimum of the two critic targets with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, governed by a single scalar coefficient. The coefficient interpolates between the pessimistic hard minimum and the neutral mean, trading off residual overestimation against the underestimation that the hard minimum can introduce, and is selected per environment by validation return. All other TD3 components — twin critics, target policy smoothing, delayed policy updates — and the Double-DQN-style decoupling of selection from evaluation are retained unchanged.
items.sig: We reframe the choice between overestimation and underestimation in off-policy value learning as a continuous bias that can be dialed, rather than a fixed commitment baked into the algorithm. TD3's clipped double-Q learning is extended by replacing the minimum of the two critic targets with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, controlled by a single scalar coefficient that interpolates between the pessimistic hard minimum and the neutral mean — directly trading residual overestimation against the underestimation the hard minimum is known to induce, with the operating point selected per environment by validation return. Twin critics, target policy smoothing, delayed policy updates, and the Double-DQN-style decoupling of selection from evaluation are all retained unchanged, so the hard minimum is recovered as one endpoint of the spectrum this estimator spans. Casting the estimation-bias trade-off as a single principled control axis offers a unifying view of a design decision that has largely been made by fiat across value-based methods.
items.pad: Borrow the Hurwicz criterion from decision theory under ambiguity, which grades an agent's stance between full pessimism (maximin) and neutrality through a single coefficient of pessimism, and read the two critics as an ambiguity set over value estimates. Under this lens, extend TD3's clipped double-Q learning by replacing the minimum of the two critic targets with a convex combination of their minimum and their mean, governed by a single scalar coefficient of pessimism. The coefficient interpolates between the ambiguity-averse hard minimum and the ambiguity-neutral mean, trading off residual overestimation against the underestimation that the hard minimum can introduce, and is selected per environment by validation return. All other TD3 components — twin critics, target policy smoothing, delayed policy updates — and the Double-DQN-style decoupling of selection from evaluation are retained unchanged.

items.prior_work: Trust-region policy optimization (TRPO; Schulman et al., 2015) constrains each policy update by bounding the KL divergence between the old and new policy, solving the resulting constrained problem via the natural-gradient direction and a backtracking line search. Proximal policy optimization (PPO; Schulman et al., 2017) replaces the hard KL constraint with a clipped surrogate objective that limits the probability ratio to a fixed interval set by a scalar clip parameter. In both methods the effective trust-region size — the KL bound or the clip parameter — is a fixed hyperparameter, tuned before training and held constant throughout. Advantages are typically estimated with generalized advantage estimation.
items.base: Replace the fixed clip parameter of the PPO surrogate objective with a scalar adjusted online by a feedback controller. Each iteration, the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy is measured and compared to a target value; the clip parameter (equivalently the trust-region size) is enlarged when the measured divergence falls below target and reduced when it exceeds target. The target value and the controller gain are hyperparameters. The remainder of the algorithm, including advantage estimation and the form of the surrogate objective, is unchanged, yielding a trust-region size that tracks a target divergence rather than remaining constant.
items.sig: We close the loop on PPO's trust region, converting a quantity ordinarily fixed by hand into a controlled variable that regulates policy displacement online. The fixed clip parameter of the PPO surrogate objective is replaced by a scalar governed by a feedback controller: at each iteration the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy is measured against a target, and the clip parameter — equivalently, the size of the trust region — is enlarged when the divergence falls below target and contracted when it exceeds it. The target divergence and the controller gain become the operative hyperparameters, while advantage estimation and the form of the surrogate objective are left untouched, so the trust-region size now tracks a target divergence instead of remaining constant. Framing step-size selection as a closed-loop control problem lends the method a principled, control-theoretic footing and points toward policy-gradient training whose stability is actively regulated rather than presumed.
items.pad: Recast trust-region sizing as a closed-loop regulation problem in the sense of classical cybernetics — a servomechanism, like a Watt governor, that holds a plant at its setpoint despite disturbances — where the plant is the policy update and the regulated quantity is its step size in the information geometry of the policy manifold. Concretely, replace the fixed clip parameter of the PPO surrogate objective with a scalar adjusted online by a feedback controller. Each iteration, the empirical KL divergence between the previous and updated policy — a distance in policy space — is measured and compared to a target value; the clip parameter (equivalently the trust-region size) is enlarged when the measured divergence falls below target and reduced when it exceeds target, exactly as an error-driven governor opens or closes to track its setpoint. The target value and the controller gain are hyperparameters. The remainder of the algorithm, including advantage estimation and the form of the surrogate objective, is unchanged, yielding a trust-region size that tracks a target divergence rather than remaining constant.

items.prior_work: Conservative Q-Learning (Kumar et al., 2020) addresses value overestimation in offline reinforcement learning by adding a penalty to the Bellman objective that lowers Q-values on out-of-distribution actions while raising Q-values on actions present in the dataset, yielding a lower bound on the true value function. This targets a central difficulty in offline RL: bootstrapping from a fixed dataset queries the Q-function on actions outside the data support, and the resulting extrapolation errors accumulate through Bellman backups. Related distribution-shift analyses and policy-constraint methods such as BCQ and BEAR restrict the learned policy or value estimates to the neighborhood of the behavior distribution. A single global coefficient controls the trade-off between conservatism and return.
items.base: Introduce a state-dependent coefficient for the conservative Q-learning penalty. Estimate local data density or epistemic uncertainty at each state using ensemble disagreement or a kernel-density model over the dataset, and scale the penalty that lowers out-of-distribution action-values in proportion to this uncertainty. Well-covered states receive a weaker penalty and sparsely-covered states a stronger one. The value lower-bound and the overall training objective are unchanged; the fixed conservatism weight is replaced by a per-state schedule tied to dataset coverage.
items.sig: A core limitation of conservative Q-learning is that a single global conservatism weight treats every state alike, penalizing out-of-distribution action-values just as harshly in densely-covered regions, where the data already pins down the value, as in sparsely-covered ones. We resolve this coupling by making the conservatism coefficient a principled function of local dataset support. At each state we estimate local data density or epistemic uncertainty, via ensemble disagreement or a kernel-density model over the dataset, and scale the penalty that lowers out-of-distribution action-values in proportion to this uncertainty, so that well-covered states are penalized weakly and sparsely-covered states strongly. Crucially, the value lower-bound guarantee and the overall training objective are preserved intact; only the fixed conservatism weight gives way to a per-state schedule tied directly to coverage. This recasts conservatism as an adaptive, uncertainty-aware quantity and opens a route to offline value estimation that respects the heterogeneous reliability of the underlying data.
items.pad: Borrowing from the physics of inhomogeneous media, promote the conservative Q-learning penalty coefficient from a global constant to a spatially varying coupling field defined across the state manifold, analogous to a position-dependent potential in a disordered material. At each state, gauge the local degree of 'disorder' through epistemic uncertainty, read off either from the fluctuation-style disagreement of an ensemble of value functions or from a kernel-density estimate of how densely the offline dataset populates that region of state space. In the spirit of screening arguments and adaptive-mesh refinement, scale the term that suppresses out-of-distribution action-values in direct proportion to this local uncertainty, so that data-rich, well-screened regions experience a weak coupling while data-poor, bare regions experience a strong one. The variational value lower-bound and the overall training objective remain exactly as before; only the single global conservatism constant is promoted to an inhomogeneous per-state field indexed by dataset coverage.

items.prior_work: Reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), as implemented in InstructGPT (Ouyang et al., 2022), fine-tunes a language model against a learned reward model using PPO, with a KL-divergence penalty to the supervised reference policy to limit drift. Gao et al. (2023) characterized reward-model overoptimization in this setup: as the policy is optimized against a proxy reward model, gold-standard reward first rises and then falls (Goodhart's law), and they fit scaling laws relating this gap to the KL distance traveled and to reward-model size and data. In standard pipelines the KL penalty coefficient is fixed or lightly tuned, and the reference policy is held constant throughout training.
items.base: Fit the overoptimization scaling-law form of Gao et al. online during PPO training, using a small held-out set of gold-labeled prompts to estimate the KL distance at which projected gold reward peaks. Use this estimate to set and adjust the KL penalty coefficient with a feedback controller, so training settles near the predicted turnover point rather than at a fixed KL budget, and allocate a larger KL allowance to prompt clusters whose fitted turnover occurs later. Evaluate against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute.
items.sig: Reward overoptimization is among the most consequential failure modes in RLHF, yet standard PPO manages it with a fixed KL budget chosen blind to where reward actually turns over. We close this gap by putting the overoptimization scaling law of Gao et al. to work as a live control signal. During PPO training we fit that scaling-law form online, using a small held-out set of gold-labeled prompts to estimate the KL distance at which projected gold reward peaks, and feed this estimate to a feedback controller that sets and adjusts the KL penalty coefficient, so training settles near the predicted turnover point rather than at an arbitrary fixed budget. The same fitted structure lets us direct KL allowance where it pays off, granting a larger allowance to prompt clusters whose fitted turnover occurs later. Measured against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute, the approach converts a descriptive account of overoptimization into an operational lever on it, with direct implications for how reward-model exploitation is managed at scale.
items.pad: Cast reward-model overoptimization as a tipping-point phenomenon in a driven dynamical system: as the policy is pushed along the KL 'reaction coordinate,' the gold objective traces an inverted-U whose apex is a critical point beyond which the system decays, a Goodhart-style bifurcation. Treat the empirical overoptimization curve of Gao et al. as an equation of state and fit its functional form online during PPO from a small held-out panel of gold-labeled prompts, yielding a running estimate of the critical KL coordinate at which projected gold reward is maximized. Close a homeostatic feedback loop around this estimate, in the manner of biological set-point regulation, that continuously tunes the KL penalty coefficient so the trajectory relaxes toward the predicted critical point rather than toward a fixed KL budget, and grant a larger KL 'energy' allowance to prompt clusters whose fitted critical point lies farther out. Benchmark against fixed-coefficient PPO on gold reward at matched compute.

items.prior_work: Random Network Distillation (Burda et al. 2019) trains a predictor network to match the embeddings produced by a fixed, randomly initialized target network; the predictor's error on a state is high for rarely visited states and is added to the extrinsic reward as an intrinsic novelty bonus. Curiosity-driven exploration (Pathak et al. 2017) learns a forward dynamics model in a feature space derived from an inverse dynamics objective, and uses the model's next-state prediction error as the intrinsic reward. Both approaches drive agents toward states where a learned model predicts poorly, and both compute the bonus from single-step prediction error over observation-derived representations, which is known to be susceptible to irreducible environment stochasticity (the "noisy-TV" problem).
items.base: Extend Random Network Distillation by replacing its single predictor–target pair with an ensemble of K independently initialized predictor–target pairs, and define the intrinsic bonus as the mean prediction error across the ensemble. Normalize this averaged error with a running estimate of its standard deviation, and linearly anneal the intrinsic-reward coefficient toward zero over training. Averaging across the ensemble reduces the variance of the novelty estimate, and annealing shifts the policy from exploration toward exploitation as extrinsic reward is discovered. The method is otherwise identical to standard RND and adds two hyperparameters, the ensemble size and the anneal schedule.
items.sig: Random Network Distillation's exploration signal rests on a single predictor–target pair, leaving its intrinsic bonus hostage to the variance of one arbitrary initialization, a well-recognized source of instability in exploration bonuses. We place that signal on firmer statistical footing. In place of the lone pair we use an ensemble of K independently initialized predictor–target pairs and define the intrinsic bonus as the mean prediction error across the ensemble, normalized by a running estimate of its standard deviation; the intrinsic-reward coefficient is then linearly annealed toward zero over training. Averaging across the ensemble reduces the variance of the novelty estimate, while annealing effects a principled handoff from exploration toward exploitation as extrinsic reward is discovered. The method leaves the rest of RND untouched, adding only two interpretable hyperparameters, the ensemble size and the anneal schedule, which makes it a clean, drop-in strengthening of one of the most widely used exploration methods.
items.pad: Recast the prediction-error novelty signal as a noisy measurement problem in the language of estimation theory and statistical physics. A single random-feature 'probe' — a predictor regressed onto a fixed, randomly initialized target embedding — furnishes just one high-variance sample of a state's surprisal. Deploy instead a replica ensemble of K independently initialized probe–target pairs and define the intrinsic bonus as the ensemble-mean prediction error, invoking the canonical variance-reduction argument (the estimator's fluctuations shrink like one over the square root of K) to obtain a cleaner novelty measurement. Standardize this averaged surprisal against a running estimate of its own standard deviation, and impose a simulated-annealing-style cooling schedule that linearly drives the intrinsic-reward coupling to zero, so behavior migrates from exploratory diffusion toward greedy exploitation as extrinsic signal is uncovered. The procedure is otherwise identical to the single-probe baseline and introduces exactly two control parameters, the replica count and the cooling schedule.

items.prior_work: Flow matching (Lipman et al. 2023) trains continuous normalizing flows by regressing a neural velocity field onto the vector field of a prescribed conditional probability path that connects a Gaussian prior to data, avoiding costly simulation during training. Rectified flow (Liu et al. 2023) instantiates this with linear interpolation between noise and data samples and introduces a reflow procedure that iteratively re-couples samples to straighten trajectories, enabling few-step ODE sampling. Both use an independent coupling between prior and data samples and generate by integrating a deterministic ODE. Mode coverage — whether the generator places mass on all data modes rather than dropping some — is evaluated with metrics such as recall or the number of captured modes.
items.base: During conditional flow matching training, weight each regression target by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, so that samples in low-density and under-represented modes contribute proportionally larger gradients. The density is estimated with a k-nearest-neighbor or kernel estimate on a held-out data subset, and the weighting exponent is a hyperparameter tuned on a recall metric. The scheme is applied on top of standard flow matching and rectified flow and leaves the ODE sampler unchanged, with evaluation by recall and captured-mode counts.
items.sig: Mode coverage, not sample fidelity, is the axis on which modern generative models most often come up short, and flow matching inherits this weakness because its uniform regression objective lets abundant modes dominate the gradient. We target the problem at its source, in the training signal itself. During conditional flow matching training, each regression target is weighted by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, so that samples in low-density and under-represented modes contribute proportionally larger gradients; the density is obtained from a k-nearest-neighbor or kernel estimate on a held-out data subset, and a single weighting exponent, tuned on a recall metric, governs the strength of the correction. The scheme sits directly on top of standard flow matching and rectified flow and leaves the ODE sampler entirely unchanged, so its coverage gains come at no inference-time cost, a property we quantify through recall and captured-mode counts. In doing so it recasts local density estimation as an explicit lever on the recall-fidelity trade-off that governs these models.
items.pad: Approach mode coverage as a biodiversity-preservation problem drawn from quantitative ecology, where the goal is to keep rare 'species' — the under-populated data modes — from going locally extinct under a sampler that favors abundant ones. During conditional flow-matching training, apply an inverse-propensity reweighting to each velocity regression target, in the spirit of rarefaction corrections and importance-weighted survey estimators, scaling its contribution by an estimate of the inverse local data density at its data endpoint, so that specimens sampled from sparse, under-represented modes exert proportionally larger pull on the learned field. The local density is read off with a k-nearest-neighbor or kernel estimate over a held-out census of the data, and the reweighting exponent is a single knob tuned against a recall (mode-retention) metric. The correction rides on top of standard flow matching and rectified flow and leaves the deterministic ODE integrator untouched, with success gauged by recall and the tally of captured modes.

items.prior_work: Elhage et al. (2022), in "Toy Models of Superposition," showed that neural networks can represent more features than they have dimensions by placing them in superposition — non-orthogonal, overlapping directions — a strategy that becomes favorable when features are sparse and differ in importance. This motivated the use of sparse dictionary learning, and specifically sparse autoencoders (SAEs), to decompose model activations into a larger set of approximately monosemantic features (Bricken et al. 2023). The standard SAE assumes a linear, additive generative model: activations are a sparse weighted sum of fixed dictionary atoms, recovered by an encoder-decoder trained with an L1 sparsity penalty. This has become a common tool for interpreting features in small transformers.
items.base: Replace the uniform L1 sparsity penalty in a standard sparse autoencoder with a per-atom penalty weighted by an estimate of each feature's importance, following the toy-model finding that feature importance governs how features are packed into superposition. Importance is estimated from the variance an atom explains in downstream activations or from its measured effect on the loss, so that more important atoms receive a smaller penalty and are less likely to be suppressed. The autoencoder is trained on residual-stream activations of a small transformer, and feature recovery is evaluated against known ground-truth features in the toy setting and by reconstruction and interpretability metrics on the model.
items.sig: Sparse autoencoders have become a primary instrument for extracting interpretable features from language models, yet they apply a uniform L1 penalty that is blind to how much any given feature matters, penalizing the load-bearing directions exactly as hard as the negligible ones. We bring the SAE objective into contact with a known result from the theory of superposition: that a feature's importance governs how it is packed into a model's representation. Concretely, we replace the uniform penalty with a per-atom penalty weighted by an estimate of each feature's importance, read off from the variance an atom explains in downstream activations or from its measured effect on the loss, so that more important atoms receive a smaller penalty and are correspondingly less likely to be suppressed. Trained on the residual-stream activations of a small transformer and evaluated against known ground-truth features in the toy setting as well as by reconstruction and interpretability metrics on the model itself, the method aligns the sparsity prior with the geometry of superposition and gives a principled account of which features a dictionary should be reluctant to drop.
items.pad: Frame sparse dictionary recovery as an optimal bit-allocation problem from rate–distortion theory, in which the L1 sparsity penalty plays the role of a uniform 'coding tax' levied on every active atom. Replace this flat tax with a variable-rate schedule — a water-filling-style allocation — in which each atom's penalty is discounted in proportion to an estimate of its importance, so that high-value channels are taxed lightly and are less likely to be quantized away, exactly as an efficient code spends more bits on the components that carry more of the signal. Importance is metered either from the variance an atom accounts for in downstream activations or from its measured contribution to the loss. The encoder–decoder is fit to residual-stream activations of a small transformer, and recovery is scored against known ground-truth generative factors in the toy regime and by reconstruction and interpretability metrics on the model.

items.prior_work: Olsson et al. (2022) identified induction heads, a class of attention heads implementing a prefix-matching and copying operation: given a partial repetition of an earlier sequence, they attend from the current token back to the token that followed its previous occurrence and predict that token will recur. The authors argued that induction heads are a primary mechanism underlying in-context learning in transformer language models, supporting this with six lines of evidence, including the coincidence of induction-head formation with a phase change in in-context learning loss during training. This established induction heads as a canonical example in mechanistic interpretability.
items.base: A generalization of the prefix-matching score used to identify induction heads that credits soft matches based on token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context, rather than exact single-token identity. Heads scoring high on this generalized metric perform approximate copying and are treated as distinct from exact-match induction heads. Their causal contribution to few-shot in-context task accuracy is assessed by targeted ablation on inputs containing paraphrased or noisy repetitions, and their appearance is tracked relative to the in-context learning phase transition during training.
items.sig: The prefix-matching score that anchors the standard account of induction heads presumes exact single-token identity, leaving unexamined a broad and consequential class of copying behavior that operates over meaning rather than surface form. We develop a graded generalization of this score that credits soft matches according to token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context, isolating the heads that perform approximate copying and situating them as a functionally separate population from exact-match induction heads. The stakes of this distinction are made directly testable: we quantify the causal role of these heads in few-shot in-context task accuracy through targeted ablation on inputs carrying paraphrased or noisy repetitions, and we trace their emergence against the in-context learning phase transition over the course of training. In doing so, the metric reframes induction as a spectrum of matching strictness and opens a tractable route to understanding how semantic, rather than literal, copying underwrites in-context generalization.
items.pad: Borrowing the vocabulary of kernel-based associative memory and signal-detection theory, we recast the discrete prefix-matching detector as a graded template-completion operator of the kind long studied in Hopfield-style attractor dynamics and in hippocampal accounts of fuzzy, content-addressable recall. Concretely, this is a generalization of the prefix-matching score used to identify induction heads that credits soft matches based on token-embedding similarity and short n-gram context, rather than exact single-token identity -- a cross-correlation-style similarity kernel replacing the delta-function of exact lookup. Heads scoring high on this generalized metric perform approximate copying and are treated as distinct from exact-match induction heads, forming a separate population of graded-completion units. Their causal contribution to few-shot in-context task accuracy is assessed by targeted ablation -- a lesioning probe in the tradition of neuropsychological deficit mapping -- on inputs containing paraphrased or noisy repetitions, and the developmental emergence of these units is tracked relative to the in-context learning phase transition during training, treated as an order-parameter-style critical point.

items.prior_work: Meng et al. (2022) introduced causal tracing, an activation-patching method that localizes where a language model stores factual associations. The procedure corrupts the subject tokens with noise, then restores clean hidden activations at individual layer and token positions to measure which sites recover the correct factual prediction. This analysis identified mid-layer feed-forward (MLP) modules at the last subject token as the primary mediators of factual recall, motivating the ROME weight-editing method that modifies those modules to change specific facts. Causal tracing and activation patching have since become standard tools for locating and editing factual knowledge in transformers.
items.base: Apply the standard corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol to a larger battery of factual relations and across several model sizes. Subject tokens are corrupted with Gaussian noise, and clean activations are restored at each layer and token position to measure the indirect effect on the correct object's probability. Map how the peak mediating layer and token position vary with relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, and report whether the mid-layer MLP localization observed for GPT-2 continues to hold across these expanded conditions.
items.sig: The mid-layer MLP localization of factual recall, established largely from a single model on a narrow set of relations, now functions as a load-bearing assumption across interpretability and model-editing research, yet its generality has never been tested at the scale where it matters. We subject this picture to a systematic stress test, applying the corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol across a substantially broader battery of factual relations and a full ladder of model sizes. Subject tokens are perturbed with Gaussian noise and clean activations are restored at each layer and token position to recover the indirect effect on the correct object's probability. By charting how the peak mediating layer and token position move as functions of relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, we establish whether the GPT-2 localization result is a stable regularity or an artifact of limited scale and coverage, a determination with immediate consequences for where knowledge-editing interventions ought to be aimed.
items.pad: Treating the network as a dynamical medium through which a factual signal propagates, we frame the study as an impulse-response, Green's-function-style probe of the sort used in linear-response analysis and in lesion-deficit mapping in systems neuroscience. We apply the standard corrupt-and-restore causal tracing protocol to a larger battery of factual relations and across several model sizes. Subject tokens are corrupted with Gaussian noise -- the injected impulse -- and clean activations are restored at each layer and token position to measure the indirect effect on the correct object's probability, exactly the mediated quantity familiar from formal mediation analysis and epidemiological source attribution. We then map how the peak mediating layer and token position -- the epicentre of the recovered response field -- vary with relation type, relation frequency, and model scale, and report whether the mid-layer MLP localization observed for GPT-2 continues to hold across these expanded conditions.

items.prior_work: Bricken et al. (2023, "Towards Monosemanticity") train sparse autoencoders on the internal activations of a language model, decomposing them into an overcomplete dictionary of features via a reconstruction (mean-squared-error) loss combined with an L1 penalty that encourages sparse feature activation. This targets superposition, where individual neurons are polysemantic, by recovering features that are more often monosemantic and interpretable through their maximally activating examples. The method exhibits a tradeoff, controlled by the sparsity coefficient, between sparsity (which aids interpretability) and reconstruction fidelity. Feature quality is assessed largely through manual and automated inspection of activation patterns, with dead features handled by periodic resampling.
items.base: Extend the sparse autoencoder by replacing the single global L1 sparsity coefficient with a per-feature coefficient adapted online to equalize activation frequency across features, and add a decorrelation penalty between decoder columns to reduce feature splitting. Retain the standard reconstruction-plus-sparsity objective and dead-feature resampling, then sweep the sparsity target across residual-stream layers to measure how the reconstruction-fidelity and automated-interpretability curves shift at matched L0 relative to the fixed-coefficient baseline.
items.sig: Two failure modes sit at the heart of what limits sparse autoencoders as instruments for reading model internals: a small set of features that monopolize the activation budget, and the fragmentation of single concepts across many decoder directions. We target both at their source. In place of the single global L1 coefficient, we assign a per-feature coefficient that adapts online to equalize activation frequency across the dictionary, and we couple this with a decorrelation penalty between decoder columns that directly discourages feature splitting. The remainder of the training recipe, the reconstruction-plus-sparsity objective and dead-feature resampling, is deliberately held fixed so the comparison is clean: sweeping the sparsity target across residual-stream layers, we characterize how the reconstruction-fidelity and automated-interpretability curves move at matched L0 against the fixed-coefficient baseline. The design speaks to a central open question, whether the interpretability-fidelity frontier is fixed or can be advanced by giving sparsity pressure the right granularity.
items.pad: Drawing on the theory of homeostatic plasticity and efficient sensory coding, we treat each dictionary atom as a coding unit governed by a firing-rate-homeostasis set point. We extend the sparse autoencoder by replacing the single global L1 sparsity coefficient with a per-feature coefficient adapted online to equalize activation frequency across features -- a direct analogue of homeostatic set-point regulation that balances neuronal activity -- and add a decorrelation penalty between decoder columns to reduce feature splitting, in the spirit of the lateral-inhibition and redundancy-reduction long invoked to explain decorrelated codes in early visual pathways. We retain the standard reconstruction-plus-sparsity objective and dead-feature resampling, then sweep the sparsity target across residual-stream layers to measure how the reconstruction-fidelity and automated-interpretability curves shift at matched L0 relative to the fixed-coefficient baseline.

items.prior_work: Attention rollout (Abnar & Zuidema 2020) produces token-level attribution maps for transformers by multiplying the per-layer attention matrices across depth, with an adjustment that accounts for residual connections. The "attention is not explanation" debate (Jain & Wallace 2019) showed that raw attention weights are not necessarily faithful: alternative attention distributions can yield the same prediction, and attention often correlates poorly with gradient-based feature importance. Follow-up work (Wiegreffe & Pinter 2019) distinguished plausibility from faithfulness. Faithfulness is typically assessed with perturbation tests — deletion and insertion of tokens ranked by attribution — or by comparison against gradient-based attribution.
items.base: Modify attention rollout by scaling each layer's attention matrix by the L2 norm of that layer's transformed value vectors before the multiplicative cross-layer aggregation, adding a magnitude term that the standard formulation omits. Evaluate the resulting token attributions on standard faithfulness benchmarks using deletion and insertion perturbation curves, and compare against gradient-times-input, across several pretrained transformer classifiers. Report whether the value-norm scaling shifts the perturbation curves relative to unweighted rollout.
items.sig: Attention rollout treats every attended token as equally consequential, propagating raw attention weights while discarding the magnitude of what each head actually writes into the residual stream, an omission that goes to the heart of why attention-based explanations remain faithfulness-limited. We restore this missing signal by scaling each layer's attention matrix by the L2 norm of that layer's transformed value vectors prior to the multiplicative cross-layer aggregation, so that the magnitude of the value contribution, not attention weight alone, shapes the resulting attribution. We assess the consequences rigorously: token attributions are measured on established faithfulness benchmarks through deletion and insertion perturbation curves and benchmarked against gradient-times-input across several pretrained transformer classifiers, quantifying how far value-norm scaling moves the perturbation curves relative to unweighted rollout. The intervention is deliberately minimal, which sharpens rather than dilutes its significance: it isolates value magnitude as a candidate lever for closing the faithfulness gap that has constrained the practical utility of attention-based attributions.
items.pad: Viewing the transformer as a layered flow network -- a signal-flow graph whose token nodes exchange mass along attention edges, so that rollout is the series composition of per-layer transfer matrices familiar from the transfer-matrix method in statistical mechanics and from conductance-weighted transport in circuit theory -- we modify attention rollout by scaling each layer's attention matrix by the L2 norm of that layer's transformed value vectors before the multiplicative cross-layer aggregation, i.e. by endowing each edge with a conductance/capacity term that the standard, purely stochastic formulation omits. We evaluate the resulting token attributions on standard faithfulness benchmarks using deletion and insertion perturbation curves, and compare against gradient-times-input, across several pretrained transformer classifiers. We report whether the value-norm (conductance-weighted) scaling shifts the perturbation curves relative to unweighted rollout.

items.prior_work: Multi-query attention (Shazeer, 2019) shares a single key and value head across all query heads, and grouped-query attention (Ainslie et al., 2023) interpolates by having groups of query heads share key/value heads; both reduce the memory footprint of the KV cache. A complementary line of work bounds cache size during long-context decoding through eviction, dropping stored key/value entries according to accumulated attention scores, recency, or heavy-hitter heuristics (e.g., StreamingLLM, H2O, SnapKV). These methods either narrow the cache across heads or shorten it across tokens while keeping a token-aligned representation. Together they form the standard toolkit for reducing KV-cache cost in long-context inference.
items.base: Allocate a single fixed total KV-cache budget across layers and heads instead of a uniform per-head keep-size. During decoding, maintain an online estimate of each head's attention dispersion (the entropy of its recent attention distribution). Heads whose attention is concentrated on few positions receive smaller budgets; heads with dispersed attention receive larger budgets, holding the total constant. Within each head, the retained key/value set is still chosen exactly as in standard eviction, by accumulated attention score together with a recency window. Only the per-head split of the fixed budget is made adaptive.
items.sig: Uniform per-head cache budgets rest on an implicit premise that every attention head compresses equally well, a premise increasingly at odds with what limits long-context inference in practice, where memory is the binding constraint and heads differ sharply in how they distribute attention. We reallocate a single fixed total KV-cache budget across layers and heads according to a principled signal of compressibility: an online estimate of each head's attention dispersion, measured as the entropy of its recent attention distribution. Heads whose attention concentrates on a few positions are the more compressible and receive smaller budgets, while heads with diffuse attention receive larger ones, with the global budget held exactly constant. Importantly, the within-head machinery is left untouched, the retained key/value set is still selected by accumulated attention score together with a recency window, so any gains are attributable to the allocation itself. By making only the per-head split adaptive, the method exposes budget geometry as an orthogonal and underexploited axis of KV-cache compression, one that composes cleanly with the eviction rules already in wide use.
items.pad: Casting cache retention as a constrained resource-allocation problem -- the cache as a conserved quantity to be partitioned like transmit power across parallel channels in the water-filling solution of information theory, or like a fixed reservoir apportioned among competing consumers in thermodynamic partitioning -- we allocate a single fixed total KV-cache budget across layers and heads instead of a uniform per-head keep-size. During decoding, we maintain an online estimate of each head's attention dispersion, quantified as the Shannon entropy of its recent attention distribution and read as that head's demand signal. Heads whose attention is concentrated on few positions (low-entropy, low-demand) receive smaller budgets; heads with dispersed, high-entropy attention receive larger budgets, holding the total conserved. Within each head, the retained key/value set is still chosen exactly as in standard eviction, by accumulated attention score together with a recency window. Only the per-head split of the fixed budget is made adaptive.

items.prior_work: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive generation by using a small draft model to propose several tokens, which the large target model then verifies in a single parallel forward pass (Leviathan et al. 2023; Chen et al. 2023). A rejection-sampling acceptance test decides how many proposed tokens to keep, and it is constructed so that the accepted output is distributed exactly as if it had been sampled from the target model. The realized speedup is governed by the acceptance rate—the expected number of tokens kept per verification step—so subsequent work has concentrated on raising it, chiefly by training or distilling the draft model to better match the target's next-token distribution.
items.base: An online controller sets the speculation length—the number of tokens drafted before each verification step—from a running estimate of the recent acceptance rate. The controller maintains an exponential moving average of accepted-token counts per step and chooses the next draft length to minimize expected wasted computation, drafting more tokens when recent acceptance has been high and fewer when it has been low. The draft and target models, the rejection-sampling acceptance test, and the exactness guarantee are all unchanged; only the per-step number of speculative tokens is set adaptively rather than being held fixed.
items.sig: We recast the speculation length not as a fixed hyperparameter but as a control variable to be regulated online, bringing a control-theoretic lens to speculative decoding that the distillation-centric literature has left largely unexplored. A lightweight online controller closes the loop between observed acceptance behavior and the drafting horizon: it tracks an exponential moving average of accepted-token counts per verification step and selects the next draft length so as to minimize expected wasted computation—lengthening the horizon when recent acceptance runs high and shortening it when acceptance falls. Because the intervention lives entirely at the scheduling layer, the draft and target models, the rejection-sampling acceptance test, and the exactness guarantee are all preserved untouched; only the per-step speculation budget becomes a dynamically regulated quantity rather than a static setting. This framing situates acceptance-rate optimization within the mature machinery of adaptive control and positions draft-length scheduling as a complementary axis of speedup—orthogonal to, and freely composable with, the draft-model-training efforts that have dominated the field.
items.pad: Borrowing the logic of optimal foraging theory from behavioral ecology, treat each verification step as a forager deciding how long to exploit a depleting patch before paying the fixed 'travel cost' of moving on. Cast as a feedback-control problem in this cybernetic idiom, an online controller sets the speculation length—the number of tokens drafted before each verification step—from a running estimate of the recent acceptance rate, the analogue of the patch's instantaneous intake rate. Exactly as the marginal-value theorem prescribes matching residence time to a running sense of environmental quality, the controller maintains an exponential moving average of accepted-token counts per step and chooses the next draft length to minimize expected wasted computation, drafting more tokens when recent acceptance has been high (a rich patch) and fewer when it has been low (a depleted one). The draft and target models, the rejection-sampling acceptance test, and the exactness guarantee are all unchanged; only the per-step number of speculative tokens is set adaptively, by this closed-loop feedback rule, rather than being held fixed.

items.prior_work: FlashAttention (Dao et al., 2022) computes exact attention with an IO-aware, tiled algorithm that keeps the N×N score matrix out of GPU high-bandwidth memory by streaming key/value blocks through on-chip SRAM using online softmax, with activation recomputation in the backward pass. To handle batches of unequal sequence length without padding waste, variable-length ("varlen") kernels pack sequences contiguously and use cumulative sequence-length offsets so each query-block attends only within its own sequence. In these kernels a grid of program instances is launched statically over (sequence, head, query-block) indices, and each instance is bound to a fixed range of the packed buffer. This static, boundary-aligned launch is standard practice in current attention-kernel libraries.
items.base: Before invoking the existing variable-length kernel, sort the sequences in each batch into length buckets and reorder the packed buffer accordingly, storing an inverse permutation to restore the original order after the kernel returns. Grouping similar-length sequences makes the work carried by concurrently launched query-blocks more uniform and collects ragged, partially-filled tail tiles together, reducing load imbalance across streaming multiprocessors. The attention kernel, its tiling, and its online-softmax computation are unchanged; only the host-side packing order and an index remapping are added, and outputs remain exact.
items.sig: Attention throughput on ragged batches is limited less by arithmetic than by how unevenly work is spread across streaming multiprocessors—a scheduling dimension that current IO-aware kernel libraries leave to a static, boundary-aligned launch. We address it at the cheapest possible layer: a host-side reordering pass that sorts each batch's sequences into length buckets and permutes the packed buffer accordingly, retaining an inverse permutation to restore the original order once the kernel returns. Concentrating similar-length sequences equalizes the work carried by concurrently launched query-blocks and consolidates ragged, partially-filled tail tiles, directly targeting the load imbalance that erodes utilization on skewed length distributions. The kernel itself—its tiling and its online-softmax computation—is left entirely intact; the method adds only a packing permutation and its inverse and keeps outputs exact, so it composes transparently with any existing varlen implementation. By isolating sequence ordering as an independent lever on kernel efficiency, the approach opens a data-layout axis for attention acceleration that complements, rather than competes with, kernel-level optimization.
items.pad: Recast the launch as a classical load-balancing problem from scheduling theory in operations research: a set of jobs with heterogeneous processing times must be assigned to identical parallel machines (the streaming multiprocessors) so as to minimize makespan and the fragmentation left by partially filled work units. In the spirit of longest-processing-time list scheduling and warehouse order-batching, before invoking the existing variable-length kernel, sort the sequences in each batch into length buckets and reorder the packed buffer accordingly, storing an inverse permutation to restore the original order after the kernel returns. Grouping similar-length sequences—an entropy-reducing rearrangement of the workload—makes the work carried by concurrently launched query-blocks more uniform and collects ragged, partially-filled tail tiles together, reducing load imbalance across streaming multiprocessors. The attention kernel, its tiling, and its online-softmax computation are unchanged; only the host-side packing order and an index remapping are added, and outputs remain exact.

items.prior_work: The neural tangent kernel (Jacot et al. 2018) shows that as network width tends to infinity, gradient-descent training behaves like kernel regression with a fixed kernel determined at initialization: parameters move negligibly and internal features do not evolve. Chizat et al. (2019) formalized this as the "lazy" regime and contrasted it with a "rich" feature-learning regime, showing that a scaling factor controls which regime training enters. Finite-width networks depart from the infinite-width NTK prediction, and characterizing when and how feature learning emerges at finite width remains an open question.
items.base: Define a width-indexed drift statistic equal to the relative Frobenius-norm change of the empirical neural tangent kernel between initialization and convergence.
