Measure this drift across a range of widths and standard architectures, fit its dependence on width, and test whether it decreases at an approximately inverse-width rate. Then relate the drift to the discrepancy between the test error predicted by kernel regression with the initial NTK and the actual test error of the trained finite-width network, assessing whether larger drift accompanies a larger prediction discrepancy.
items.sig: The gap between the infinite-width NTK's fixed-kernel prediction and the feature learning that finite networks actually exhibit remains one of the least quantified aspects of deep-learning theory, largely for want of a single measurable order parameter. We supply one: a width-indexed drift statistic defined as the relative Frobenius-norm change of the empirical neural tangent kernel from initialization to convergence—a scalar that captures how far a finite network departs from lazy training. Measured across a range of widths and standard architectures and fit as a function of width, the statistic lets us test directly whether kernel motion vanishes at the theoretically anticipated inverse-width rate, turning an asymptotic statement into a falsifiable finite-width prediction. We then tie the statistic to what practitioners ultimately care about: the discrepancy between the test error predicted by kernel regression under the initial NTK and the realized test error of the trained finite-width network, assessing whether greater drift systematically tracks greater predictive failure of the lazy approximation. The outcome is a calibrated, empirically grounded diagnostic that connects the lazy/rich dichotomy to concrete generalization behavior and provides a principled instrument for charting where feature learning switches on as width varies.
items.pad: Viewed through the lens of finite-size scaling in statistical mechanics, the empirical neural tangent kernel plays the role of a response function whose flow away from its initial value measures the breakdown of the mean-field (infinite-width) description as one departs the thermodynamic limit. Define a width-indexed drift statistic—an order parameter for this departure—equal to the relative Frobenius-norm change of the empirical neural tangent kernel between initialization and convergence. Measure this drift across a range of widths and standard architectures, fit its dependence on width, and test whether it decreases at an approximately inverse-width rate, the signature of a leading 1/N finite-size correction to a self-averaging quantity. Then relate the drift to the discrepancy between the test error predicted by kernel regression with the initial NTK—the linear-response prediction of the mean-field theory—and the actual test error of the trained finite-width network, assessing whether larger drift accompanies a larger prediction discrepancy.

items.prior_work: Soudry et al. (2018) analyzed gradient descent on linearly separable data trained with the logistic loss and other exponentially-tailed losses. They showed that although the loss is driven to zero and the parameter norm diverges, the normalized parameters converge in direction to the L2 max-margin separator (the hard-margin SVM solution), independently of initialization. The convergence to this direction is slow, at an O(1/log t) rate. This established that gradient descent carries an implicit bias toward maximum-margin solutions even without explicit regularization.
items.base: Extend the directional-convergence analysis to gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses (e.g., the logistic loss). The normalized iterate still converges in direction to the L2 max-margin (hard-margin SVM) solution, with the momentum coefficient entering only the rate constants and lower-order terms, leaving the limiting direction and the O(1/log t) convergence order unchanged. Conditions on the step size and momentum coefficient are given under which the loss tends to zero and the direction converges.
items.sig: Implicit-bias theory has so far characterized the vanilla gradient-descent trajectory, yet essentially every model at scale is trained with momentum—leaving open whether the max-margin bias that underwrites generalization guarantees survives the optimizer actually deployed. We close this gap by carrying the directional-convergence analysis through to gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses such as the logistic loss. The analysis establishes that the normalized iterate still converges in direction to the L2 max-margin (hard-margin SVM) separator, with the momentum coefficient entering only the rate constants and lower-order terms and leaving both the limiting direction and the O(1/log t) convergence order intact—an invariance indicating that the implicit bias is a property of the loss geometry rather than an artifact of the specific descent dynamics. We further supply explicit conditions on the step size and momentum coefficient under which the loss tends to zero and the direction converges, delineating the operating regime in which the guarantee holds. Beyond momentum itself, the argument marks out a route for asking which accelerated and preconditioned optimizers preserve, and which might perturb, the margin-maximizing bias.
items.pad: Read heavy-ball momentum literally as classical mechanics: a massive particle sliding through a potential landscape (the loss surface) under viscous damping, its trajectory escaping to infinity along an asymptotic ray much as a hyperbolic orbit in celestial mechanics approaches its asymptote. In this dynamical-systems idiom, extend the directional-convergence analysis to gradient descent with constant heavy-ball momentum on linearly separable data under exponentially-tailed losses (e.g., the logistic loss). The normalized iterate—the direction of the escaping trajectory—still converges in direction to the L2 max-margin (hard-margin SVM) solution, which acts as the attracting terminal direction, with the momentum coefficient (the particle's inertia relative to its damping) entering only the rate constants and lower-order terms, leaving the limiting direction and the O(1/log t) convergence order unchanged. Conditions on the step size and momentum coefficient—the discretization time step and the inertia-to-friction ratio—are given under which the loss tends to zero and the direction converges.

items.prior_work: Kaplan et al. (2020) showed that language-model test loss follows smooth power laws in model size (N), dataset size (D), and training compute (C), and used these to recommend spending most of a compute budget on model size while training on relatively few tokens. Hoffmann et al. (2022), the Chinchilla study, re-examined the compute-optimal frontier and fit a parametric loss L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β, concluding that model size and training tokens should scale in roughly equal proportion (about 20 tokens per parameter). Jointly increasing N and D as compute grows has since become the standard basis for planning large-model training runs. Both analyses treat the optimal allocation as a single static operating point chosen before training begins.
items.base: Reformulate the Chinchilla compute-optimal analysis under a total-lifetime compute budget that adds expected inference cost to training cost: C_total = C_train + C_inf, where inference compute grows with model size and with the number of tokens served by the deployed model. Using the fitted loss form L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β, minimize loss subject to the lifetime budget for a given expected query volume. Because inference cost scales with model size but not with training-token count, the constrained optimum shifts toward smaller models trained on more tokens as expected deployment volume increases. Report the corrected token-per-parameter ratio as a function of anticipated inference load.
items.sig: Current scaling-law planning optimizes a training-only compute budget, implicitly treating a model as if it were discarded the moment training ends—an assumption increasingly at odds with systems that serve enormous token volumes over their deployed lifetime. We recast the Chinchilla compute-optimal analysis around the quantity that actually governs deployment economics: a total-lifetime budget C_total = C_train + C_inf that folds expected inference cost—which grows with model size and with the number of tokens served—into the same objective as training. Retaining Chinchilla's fitted loss form L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β, we minimize loss subject to this lifetime constraint for a given expected query volume. Because inference cost scales with model size but is insensitive to the training-token count, the constrained optimum migrates toward smaller models trained on more tokens as anticipated deployment volume rises—yielding a corrected token-per-parameter ratio expressed as a function of inference load rather than a single deployment-agnostic constant. Casting allocation as a deployment-aware optimization repositions scaling laws from a pre-training accounting exercise to a lifetime-cost discipline, and gives model builders a directly actionable rule for sizing models to their expected serving regime.
items.pad: Frame the design choice as an engineering-economics life-cycle costing problem, of the kind used to size a capital asset whose up-front construction cost trades off against its operating cost over a long service life—capital expenditure versus operating expenditure amortized across the deployment lifespan. Reformulate the Chinchilla compute-optimal analysis under a total-lifetime compute budget that adds expected inference cost to training cost: C_total = C_train + C_inf, where inference compute grows with model size and with the number of tokens served by the deployed model—the operating 'metabolic' load the asset must sustain. Using the fitted loss form L(N,D)=E+A/N^α+B/D^β, minimize loss subject to the lifetime budget for a given expected query volume. Because inference cost scales with model size but not with training-token count—capital committed to a larger body incurs a proportionally larger lifelong operating burden—the constrained optimum shifts toward smaller models trained on more tokens as expected deployment volume increases, echoing how life-history theory shifts optimal adult body size under a heavier lifetime maintenance demand. Report the corrected token-per-parameter ratio as a function of anticipated inference load.

items.prior_work: Chain-of-thought prompting (Wei et al. 2022) improves large language model performance on multi-step tasks by having the model generate intermediate reasoning steps before its final answer. Subsequent work established that these generated explanations are not always faithful to the model's actual computation: Turpin et al. (2023) showed that biasing features in the prompt, such as the ordering of answer options, can drive a model's answer while remaining absent from its stated reasoning. This motivated a line of faithfulness probes that use input interventions to test whether the reported reasoning reflects the factors actually determining the output. Faithfulness is typically assessed behaviorally, by perturbing inputs or reasoning steps and observing changes in the final answer.
items.base: Extend the counterfactual biasing-cue probe into a systematic benchmark: apply a battery of input perturbations — reordering answer options, inserting a suggested answer, and rephrasing the question — that alter the model's output without appearing in its stated reasoning, and quantify unfaithfulness as the rate at which such cues flip the final answer while going unmentioned. Measure how this rate varies with model scale and task category across several model families, and report the resulting benchmark for comparison.
items.sig: We recast the counterfactual biasing-cue probe from a one-off diagnostic into a principled measurement instrument for chain-of-thought faithfulness. A coordinated battery of input perturbations — reordering answer options, inserting a suggested answer, and rephrasing the question — is used to steer the model's output through channels that never surface in its stated reasoning; unfaithfulness is then read off directly as the rate at which such silent cues flip the final answer while going unmentioned. By resolving this rate along two axes that have so far been examined largely in isolation — model scale and task category, across several model families — the benchmark turns a phenomenon usually reported anecdotally into a quantitative, comparable, and reproducible signal, and supplies the field with a shared yardstick for auditing whether a model's explanations track the computation that actually drives its answers. The resulting instrument establishes a common basis for monitoring faithfulness as models grow and as evaluation moves toward reasoning we can trust.
items.pad: Borrowing the logic of psychophysical dissociation experiments — where a covert stimulus provably drives a subject's response yet never enters their verbal report — treat the model as a black-box respondent and assemble a stimulus battery of covert perturbations: permuting the ordering of the answer options, injecting a planted suggested answer, and paraphrasing the question stem. Each perturbation is engineered to displace the emitted decision while remaining absent from the narrated justification. Framed information-theoretically as a hidden leakage channel between stimulus and response, we quantify unfaithfulness as the rate at which such covert cues flip the terminal answer without surfacing in the reasoning trace — the confabulation rate of the stated justification. We then profile how this dissociation rate scales with model capacity and stratifies across the task taxonomy over several model families, and release the resulting stimulus-response battery as a comparative instrument.

items.prior_work: Instruction tuning fine-tunes language models on collections of tasks phrased as natural-language instructions. Wei et al. (2022, FLAN) showed that tuning on a large, diverse set of tasks improves zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks, with gains associated with the number of task clusters included. Zhou et al. (2023, LIMA) reported that roughly a thousand carefully curated examples can produce strong instruction-following, framed as the 'superficial alignment' or 'less is more' hypothesis. Together these results motivate ongoing debate over whether the quantity of instruction data or the diversity and quality of its coverage is what drives generalization.
items.base: At a fixed training budget, instruction examples are selected to maximize coverage of task clusters defined in a sentence-embedding space. Instructions are embedded and clustered by similarity, and a subset is chosen so that the number of distinct clusters represented is maximized rather than sampled in proportion to cluster frequency. This coverage-maximizing selection is compared against uniform random sampling and against scaling the total example count under matched compute. The measured outcome is zero-shot generalization to held-out task families, isolating whether cluster coverage or example quantity better explains it.
items.sig: We formalize instruction-tuning data selection as an explicit coverage-maximization objective and use it to adjudicate a question that sits at the heart of the diversity-versus-quantity debate: does a model's generalization depend on how much of the task space its data touches, or simply on how many examples it sees? At a fixed training budget, instructions are embedded and clustered in a sentence-embedding space, and a subset is chosen to maximize the number of distinct clusters represented rather than sampling in proportion to cluster frequency — turning 'diversity' from an informal desideratum into an optimization target. The design deliberately pits this coverage-maximizing selection against both uniform random sampling and against scaling the total example count under matched compute, so that cluster coverage and example quantity — ordinarily entangled — are cleanly separated. With zero-shot generalization to held-out task families as the outcome, the study isolates which of the two actually drives generalization, yielding data-curation guidance that transfers directly to compute-constrained instruction tuning.
items.pad: Cast instruction curation as a biodiversity-maximization problem in the manner of community ecology: treat each task cluster as a 'species' occupying a niche in a sentence-embedding manifold, and, under a fixed sampling budget, assemble the training pool to maximize species richness — the count of distinct niches represented — rather than sampling niches in proportion to their abundance. Instructions are embedded and agglomerated into niches by similarity, and the richness-maximizing subset is contrasted with neutral (uniform-random) sampling and with merely enlarging the census by scaling the total example count under matched compute. As in rarefaction analysis, the endpoint is out-of-sample generalization — here zero-shot transfer to held-out task families — adjudicating whether niche coverage or raw census size better accounts for it.

items.prior_work: Xie et al. (2022) explain in-context learning (ICL) as implicit Bayesian inference. They model pretraining as a mixture over latent concepts, with each document generated by a hidden Markov model conditioned on a concept. Under this account, a prompt of concatenated examples shares a latent concept, and the model implicitly infers it: as the number of examples grows, the posterior concentrates on the shared concept and prediction improves, even though the prompt's concatenated format differs from coherent pretraining documents. The framework is supported by theory and by the synthetic GINC benchmark.
items.base: An extension characterizes how quickly the implicit posterior concentrates on the shared concept as a function of the statistical distinguishability between concepts in the pretraining mixture. Using divergence measures between concept-conditioned distributions, it predicts that in-context accuracy rises faster, requiring fewer examples, when the true concept is well separated from competing concepts, and more slowly when concepts overlap. A hierarchical prior over concept families is added so related concepts share structure, modeling transfer between similar tasks. Predictions are evaluated on GINC-style synthetic data with controlled concept separation.
items.sig: This work sharpens the Bayesian account of in-context learning from a statement that a posterior exists to a quantitative prediction of how fast it forms. We characterize the rate at which the implicit posterior concentrates on the shared concept as an explicit function of the statistical distinguishability between concepts in the pretraining mixture, using divergence measures between concept-conditioned distributions to derive a testable law: in-context accuracy should climb with fewer demonstrations when the true concept is well separated from its competitors, and stall when concepts overlap. Extending the prior to a hierarchical structure over concept families — so that related concepts share statistical structure — brings task transfer under the same analytical umbrella, tying sample-efficiency to the geometry of the concept space. Evaluated on GINC-style synthetic data with concept separation dialed under controlled conditions, the predictions convert an abstract inference-theoretic picture into quantities one can measure and falsify, and give a principled handle on why some in-context tasks are learned from a handful of examples while others resist.
items.pad: Recast posterior concentration as a relaxation-to-equilibrium process on a statistical manifold. Treating the concept mixture as a set of competing thermodynamic phases, we characterize how quickly the implicit posterior settles onto the shared concept as a function of the geometric separation between phases, measured by information-geometric divergences between concept-conditioned distributions. In the vocabulary of nucleation kinetics, the prediction is that in-context accuracy relaxes rapidly, needing fewer examples, when the true phase is well separated from its competitors, and exhibits critical slowing-down when phases overlap. A hierarchical prior over concept families is introduced so that kindred phases share latent structure, modeling transfer between similar tasks as shared order parameters. Predictions are evaluated on GINC-style synthetic corpora with controlled concept separation.

items.prior_work: Byte-pair encoding (Sennrich et al., 2016) builds subword vocabularies by greedily merging frequent character pairs, and has become the default tokenizer family for large language models. Subsequent work on numeric reasoning showed that how numbers are segmented matters: models trained with consistent single-digit (or fixed-length digit-chunk) tokenization arithmetic more reliably than those that inherit merged, variable-length number tokens from frequency-driven BPE. In the multilingual setting, standard BPE vocabularies allocate fewer merges to lower-resource scripts, yielding higher token-per-word fertility and degraded downstream performance for those languages. The consensus view treats tokenizer granularity as a per-corpus preprocessing choice tuned largely by compression efficiency.
items.base: Compare a fixed set of BPE granularities (varying only the merge count / vocabulary size) on a matched benchmark that spans multi-digit arithmetic and a panel of high- and low-resource languages, using a single-digit-forced number pretokenization rule as one of the arms. For each granularity, measure token fertility per language, arithmetic accuracy by operand length, and downstream multilingual task scores, then report the correlation between fertility and accuracy across the grid. The contribution is an empirical sweep quantifying how the arithmetic-optimal and multilingual-optimal granularity settings trade off within the existing BPE framework, holding model size and training data fixed.
items.sig: Tokenizer granularity is one of the least-scrutinized levers in modern language-model design, yet it silently arbitrates a tension between two capabilities the field cares about deeply: multi-digit arithmetic and equitable multilingual performance. We map this tradeoff directly. Holding model size and training data fixed, we sweep a controlled set of BPE granularities — varying only merge count / vocabulary size, with a single-digit-forced number pretokenization rule as one arm — over a matched benchmark spanning multi-digit arithmetic and a panel of high- and low-resource languages. For each setting we measure token fertility per language, arithmetic accuracy resolved by operand length, and downstream multilingual scores, and trace the correlation between fertility and accuracy across the grid, positioning fertility as the quantity that may mediate the link between granularity and capability. The result is a quantitative characterization of where the arithmetic-optimal and multilingual-optimal settings diverge within the existing BPE framework — turning a choice usually made by default into an evidence-based design frontier, and giving practitioners a principled basis for a granularity decision they currently make largely by inertia.
items.pad: Frame tokenizer granularity as a coarse-graining resolution in the spirit of the renormalization group and rate-distortion theory: the merge count sets the scale at which the symbol stream is compressed, and we ask how observables flow as this scale is swept. Holding model size and training corpus fixed, we sweep a fixed ladder of BPE resolutions (varying only the merge count / vocabulary size) across a matched battery spanning multi-digit arithmetic and a panel of high- and low-resource scripts, with a single-digit-forced number pretokenization rule as one rung. At each resolution we record the per-language coding cost (token fertility per word), arithmetic accuracy stratified by operand length, and downstream multilingual scores, then report the fertility-accuracy correlation across the ladder. The contribution is an empirical scaling sweep charting how the arithmetic-optimal and multilingual-optimal coarse-graining scales trade off within the standard BPE codebook.

items.prior_work: CLIP (Radford et al. 2021) learns a joint image-text embedding space via contrastive pretraining on large web-scraped image-caption pairs, enabling zero-shot image classification by matching image embeddings to the text embeddings of category names. Subsequent open-vocabulary detection methods (e.g., ViLD, RegionCLIP, Detic) transfer this alignment to object detection by distilling CLIP's image encoder into region features and classifying each region against the CLIP text embeddings of candidate category names. This lets detectors recognize categories specified only by text at test time, including classes absent from the detection training labels. The dominant design represents each category as a fixed point in CLIP's text-embedding space and scores regions by cosine similarity.
items.base: Extend CLIP-based open-vocabulary detection on the text side of region-category matching. For each category, ensemble multiple prompt templates and augment the label with synonyms and hypernyms drawn from a lexical database, averaging their CLIP text embeddings into a single category representation. During training of the region classifier, sample hard-negative category names that are semantically close to the ground-truth label to sharpen the region-text similarity boundary. Region embeddings continue to be distilled from a frozen CLIP image encoder, and classification remains cosine similarity between a region feature and the aggregated category text embedding.
items.sig: Most gains in CLIP-based open-vocabulary detection have been pursued on the vision side, leaving the text side of region-category matching comparatively under-exploited. We concentrate there. Each category is represented not by a single prompt but by an ensemble of prompt templates whose label is enriched with synonyms and hypernyms drawn from a lexical database, their CLIP text embeddings averaged into one linguistically grounded category vector — letting the structure of the lexicon, rather than a lone template, define what a category means. To make these richer representations discriminative, training samples hard-negative category names that sit semantically close to the ground-truth label, sharpening exactly the region-text boundaries where confusable classes collide. The vision pathway is left untouched — region embeddings are still distilled from a frozen CLIP image encoder and classification remains a cosine similarity — so the improvement drops into existing detectors at negligible cost while pointing to the language side as a fertile and still largely open axis for scaling detection to open vocabularies.
items.pad: Reframe the category side of region-label matching through prototype theory from cognitive semantics: rather than a single lexical point, a category is rendered as a graded prototype formed from the centroid of its lexical neighborhood. For each category we ensemble multiple prompt templates and enrich the label with synonyms and superordinate (hypernym) terms harvested from a lexical ontology, then average their CLIP text embeddings — in effect taking a colimit over the small diagram of near-synonyms — into a single prototype representation. While training the region classifier, we draw hard-negative category names that are semantically adjacent in the ontology to the ground-truth label, sharpening the inter-prototype decision boundary as in contrastive discrimination among confusable concepts. Region embeddings continue to be distilled from a frozen CLIP image encoder, and classification remains cosine similarity between a region feature and the aggregated category prototype.

items.prior_work: Li et al. (2018) showed that the propagation step in graph convolutional networks is a form of repeated Laplacian smoothing, so as depth grows node representations converge toward a low-frequency, degree-dependent fixed point and become indistinguishable — the "oversmoothing" that limits deep GNNs. Subsequent work treated this as a phenomenon to be counteracted layer by layer. PairNorm (Zhao & Akoglu 2020) is a representative fix: a parameter-light normalization layer inserted between graph convolutions that rescales and re-centers features so that the total pairwise squared distance across nodes is held roughly constant with depth, keeping representations from collapsing while leaving the underlying aggregation unchanged.
items.base: Extend PairNorm so that its single global rescaling constant is replaced by a rescaling factor that varies with node degree and with layer depth. Instead of holding one target for total pairwise distance across all nodes, the normalization target is set per degree bucket and per layer, with a small number of learned coefficients fit during training. The aggregation operator is otherwise unchanged. Because high-degree nodes smooth faster under Laplacian propagation, allowing their normalization scale to differ from low-degree nodes is intended to keep the anti-collapse effect better matched across the degree distribution as depth increases.
items.sig: We reconceive anti-collapse normalization in deep GNNs as a problem of matching the correction to the heterogeneous rate at which oversmoothing acts across a single graph. The governing observation is that Laplacian propagation drives high-degree nodes toward the low-frequency fixed point faster than low-degree nodes, so any single global rescaling constant — as used in PairNorm — must under- or over-correct somewhere along the degree distribution, leaving a residual, structured collapse that compounds with depth. We therefore replace the one scalar target for total pairwise distance with a family of normalization targets indexed jointly by degree bucket and by layer depth, governed by a small set of coefficients fit during training, while leaving the aggregation operator untouched. This degree- and depth-resolved rescaling is designed to hold the anti-collapse effect uniformly matched across node populations as networks deepen, and it recasts a parameter-light normalization layer as a structure-aware regulator of representational geometry — a lens that bears directly on the broader question of how far graph networks can be scaled in depth before their capacity to separate nodes is exhausted.
items.pad: Borrowing the language of the renormalization group and of heat diffusion on disordered media, treat each propagation step as a coarse-graining move along a scale axis — with network depth playing the role of diffusion time — that drives the node ensemble toward a thermodynamic fixed point, a low-frequency condensate. In this statistical-mechanics picture a site's local coordination number, i.e. its degree, sets its local relaxation rate, so a single system-wide temperature-like rescaling cannot hold the ensemble away from the condensate uniformly across sites. Accordingly, replace the normalization layer's single global rescaling constant with a rescaling factor that varies with node degree and with layer depth: rather than pinning one target for the total pairwise squared distance across all nodes, set the normalization target per degree bucket and per scale (layer), governed by a small number of learned coefficients fit during training, while leaving the aggregation (diffusion) operator itself untouched. Because high-coordination sites relax toward the low-frequency fixed point faster under the Laplacian dynamics, granting them a scale-dependent normalization distinct from that of low-degree sites is meant to keep the anti-condensation effect matched across the full degree distribution as coarse-graining depth grows.

items.prior_work: Xu et al. (2019) showed that message-passing graph neural networks are at most as expressive as the one-dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman (1-WL) color-refinement test at distinguishing non-isomorphic graphs, and that this bound is attained when the neighborhood aggregation and readout functions are injective over multisets, as in the Graph Isomorphism Network (GIN). Morris et al. (2019) established the parallel correspondence between higher-order GNNs and the k-WL hierarchy. Together these results frame the expressive power of graph neural networks as a discrete ladder indexed by the WL hierarchy, with standard message passing pinned at the 1-WL level. Subsequent work has largely accepted this framing and sought architectures that climb the ladder.
items.base: Augment the input node features of a GIN with a fixed set of precomputed structural descriptors — for example, counts of small subgraphs such as triangles and short cycles, together with shortest-path distance encodings to a set of anchor nodes. Because these descriptors take different values on some node pairs that 1-WL colors identically, the augmented model can distinguish certain pairs of graphs that standard message passing cannot. The proposal is to specify which descriptor sets provably lift distinguishing power above 1-WL for named graph families, and to measure the resulting gains on standard graph-classification benchmarks.
items.sig: Rather than climbing the Weisfeiler-Leman ladder by redesigning the message-passing machinery, we ask what minimal, precomputable structural information injected at the input already suffices to certify separation power beyond 1-WL — and for which graph families that separation can be proven. Concretely, we augment the node features of a GIN with a fixed set of structural descriptors — counts of small subgraphs such as triangles and short cycles, together with shortest-path distance encodings to a set of anchor nodes — selected because they take distinct values on node pairs that 1-WL renders indistinguishable. The core contribution is a constructive account, resolved per named graph family, of which descriptor sets provably lift distinguishing power above the 1-WL barrier, paired with a measurement of the realized gains on standard graph-classification benchmarks. This reframes expressivity not as an intrinsic ceiling of message passing but as a property that can be dialed through a certified, theory-guided choice of input encoding, tying the combinatorial structure of specific graph families to concrete architectural decisions and opening a route to expressivity guarantees that travel with the descriptor set rather than with the network.
items.pad: Drawing on chemical graph theory and the motif-census tradition of systems biology — where molecules and biological interaction networks are fingerprinted by tallying local topological substructures — decorate each node of a Graph Isomorphism Network with a fixed vector of precomputed structural descriptors before any message passing begins. Concretely, these are counts of small subgraph motifs such as triangles and short cycles (the graph-theoretic analogue of ring-and-clique indices in cheminformatics), together with geodesic shortest-path distance encodings measured to a chosen set of anchor landmarks. Because such topological fingerprints take distinct values on certain node pairs that the color-refinement procedure renders chromatically identical, the descriptor-augmented model resolves pairs of graphs that plain neighborhood message passing conflates. The program is to characterize, for named families of graphs, exactly which descriptor sets provably raise resolving power beyond the first-order (1-WL) refinement floor, and to quantify the resulting improvements on standard graph-classification benchmarks.

items.prior_work: DP-SGD (Abadi et al., 2016) trains models under differential privacy by clipping each per-example gradient to a fixed L2 norm bound and adding isotropic Gaussian noise scaled to that bound before averaging the update. The moments accountant tracks accumulated privacy loss across training steps, yielding tighter (ε, δ) guarantees than naive composition of the per-step Gaussian mechanisms. The clipping norm and the noise multiplier are the central knobs governing the privacy-utility tradeoff: smaller clipping norms and larger noise strengthen privacy but degrade utility. This framework is the standard baseline for differentially private deep learning.
items.base: Extend DP-SGD by replacing the single global clipping norm with per-layer clipping norms, each set proportional to a running online estimate of that layer's average per-example gradient norm. Gaussian noise is calibrated separately for each layer to its assigned clipping norm, and the per-layer mechanisms are composed under the moments accountant to report a total (ε, δ). The allocation redistributes a fixed noise budget toward layers with larger gradients, aiming to reduce clipping-induced utility loss while preserving the same overall privacy guarantee as standard DP-SGD at matched total noise.
items.sig: We treat the single global clipping norm at the heart of DP-SGD as the binding constraint on private-training utility and ask what is forfeited by forcing one isotropic bound onto a gradient field whose scale varies markedly from layer to layer. Our formulation replaces that one norm with a vector of per-layer clipping norms, each set proportional to a running online estimate of its layer's average per-example gradient norm, and calibrates the Gaussian noise separately for each layer to its own bound; the resulting per-layer mechanisms are composed under the moments accountant to report a single total (ε, δ). Read as a resource-allocation problem, this redistributes a fixed noise budget toward the layers whose gradients carry the most signal, targeting the clipping-induced utility loss that dominates DP-SGD error while holding the end-to-end privacy guarantee exactly at matched total noise. Casting private optimization as layerwise budget allocation over a heterogeneous gradient geometry, rather than as a monolithic norm-and-noise operation, is directly consequential for extending differentially private training to the large, deep models where per-layer gradient scales differ by orders of magnitude and a single clipping norm is least defensible.
items.pad: Recast differentially private training as the problem of conveying a gradient signal across a bank of parallel Gaussian channels — one channel per network layer, each corrupted by additive noise — in the spirit of the water-filling power-allocation results of classical information theory. Viewed this way, the single global clipping norm acts as a uniform per-channel gain, which is wasteful when the channels carry signals of very different magnitude. The proposal replaces it with per-layer clipping norms, each set proportional to a running online estimate of that layer's average per-example gradient norm; the additive Gaussian noise is calibrated separately for each channel (layer) to its assigned clipping norm, and the per-layer mechanisms are composed under the moments accountant to report a single total (ε, δ). As in allocating a fixed transmit-power budget across subcarriers, this redistributes a fixed noise budget toward the high-magnitude layers, aiming to lower the clipping-induced distortion (utility loss) while holding the overall privacy guarantee identical to standard DP-SGD at matched total noise.

items.prior_work: Membership-inference attacks (MIAs) determine whether a specific example was part of a model's training set by exploiting differences in the model's behavior on seen versus unseen data. Shokri et al. (2017) introduced shadow-model attacks, and Carlini et al. (2022) proposed the Likelihood Ratio Attack (LiRA), which calibrates a per-example likelihood ratio against reference models trained with and without the target point, achieving high true-positive rates at low false-positive rates. MI vulnerability is widely attributed to overfitting, and standard regularizers (weight decay, dropout, data augmentation, early stopping) are known empirically to reduce attack success by shrinking the train-test generalization gap.
items.base: Run LiRA against image classifiers trained across a sweep of a single regularizer's strength (e.g., weight-decay coefficient or dropout rate), holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed. For each setting, measure the attack's true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates alongside the train-test generalization gap, and fit the resulting dose-response relationship between regularization strength and attack success. Report the functional form and identify the strength at which marginal privacy gains diminish, providing calibrated guidance for choosing regularization hyperparameters under a target attack-success budget.
items.sig: The protective effect of regularization against membership inference is widely asserted yet has been characterized only qualitatively; we set out to render it as a measured dose-response law with operational teeth. Using LiRA — the strongest attack available at low false-positive rates — against image classifiers, we sweep a single regularizer's strength (weight-decay coefficient or dropout rate) while holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed, and at each setting record the attack's true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates alongside the train-test generalization gap. Fitting the resulting curve yields an explicit functional form linking regularization strength to attack success and locates the point at which marginal privacy return diminishes. The contribution is to move membership-inference defense from a qualitative heuristic to a calibrated instrument: a curve that lets a practitioner select regularization hyperparameters against a stated attack-success budget, together with a reusable methodology for quantifying the empirical privacy purchased by any regularizer in the many settings where formal differential-privacy guarantees are unavailable or too costly to deploy.
items.pad: Treat regularization as a therapeutic dose and privacy leakage as the pharmacodynamic response, importing the exposure-response methodology of pharmacology and toxicology. Administer the Likelihood Ratio Attack to image classifiers trained across a graded titration of a single regularizer — a weight-decay coefficient or dropout rate serving as the 'dose' — while holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed as the controlled assay conditions. At each dose, read out the attack's true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates as the response endpoint, recorded alongside the train-test generalization gap as a covariate, and fit the resulting dose-response curve linking regularization strength to attack success. The aim is to report its functional form — the analogue of a Hill-type saturation curve — and to locate the point of diminishing marginal effect (a privacy analogue of an EC-style threshold), yielding calibrated guidance for selecting regularization hyperparameters against a target attack-success budget, much as a therapeutic window is chosen in dose-finding.

items.prior_work: Estimating heterogeneous (conditional average) treatment effects, or CATE, is a core problem in causal machine learning. Causal forests (Wager & Athey 2018) adapt random forests with honest sample-splitting to produce pointwise CATE estimates with asymptotic normality and valid confidence intervals. Meta-learners (Künzel et al. 2019) — the S-, T-, and X-learners — instead reduce CATE estimation to a sequence of standard supervised-regression subproblems that can be solved with any base learner. Neural-network instantiations follow the same template: shared representations with treatment-specific outcome heads and, in doubly-robust variants, propensity models used to form Neyman-orthogonal pseudo-outcomes.
items.base: Instantiate the X-learner with a single shared-trunk neural network. One representation feeds two outcome heads for the treated and control responses; the first-stage imputed individual effects are then regressed by two further heads, and the two effect estimates are combined using an estimated propensity score. An integral-probability-metric penalty balances the treated and control representations, and the whole model is trained end-to-end rather than in separate stages.
items.sig: The X-learner is among the most statistically principled meta-learners for heterogeneous treatment effects, yet its power is diluted by a stagewise construction in which each regression is fit in isolation and errors accumulate across uncoordinated steps. We collapse the entire X-learner pipeline into one jointly optimized neural architecture: a single shared trunk feeds two outcome heads for the treated and control responses, two further heads regress the first-stage imputed individual effects, and the two effect estimates are combined through an estimated propensity score — with an integral-probability-metric penalty enforcing balance between the treated and control representations and the whole system trained end-to-end. Folding representation learning, outcome modeling, effect imputation, and covariate balancing into a single differentiable objective lets a shared inductive bias be amortized across the very stages that separate estimation keeps apart, and it situates the X-learner squarely within the representation-balancing tradition of causal neural networks — a unification that points toward a general recipe for rendering multi-stage causal estimators as coherent end-to-end models.
items.pad: Frame the estimation of individual treatment effects as reconstructing an unobservable counterfactual twin — the outcome the same unit would exhibit in a parallel world where its assignment were flipped — and cast the covariate-balancing step in the language of optimal transport and domain adaptation, where treated and control populations are two shifted distributions to be aligned. Concretely, instantiate the X-learner atop a single shared-trunk network: one learned representation feeds two outcome heads for the treated and control responses; the first-stage imputed individual effects are then regressed by two further heads; and the two effect estimates are reconciled through an estimated propensity score. An integral-probability-metric penalty — an optimal-transport-flavored discrepancy between the two representation distributions — pulls the treated and control embeddings into alignment, and the entire apparatus is trained end-to-end rather than as the separate stages of the classical recipe.

items.prior_work: Double/debiased machine learning (Chernozhukov et al., 2018) estimates low-dimensional causal or structural parameters when nuisance functions—such as the propensity score and outcome regression—are high-dimensional and estimated with flexible machine learning. It combines Neyman-orthogonal moment conditions, which are first-order insensitive to nuisance estimation error, with cross-fitting, which splits the sample so each observation's moment is evaluated using nuisances fit on other observations. Under a product-rate condition on the nuisance estimators, this yields √n-consistent, asymptotically normal estimates with valid confidence intervals despite slow nuisance convergence. The framework has become a standard template for causal inference with machine-learning nuisances.
items.base: Reduce the dependence of the double machine learning estimator on the particular random cross-fitting partition by computing the orthogonal-score estimate over many independent partitions and aggregating the point estimates with a median-of-means combiner. Adjust the variance estimator to add the between-partition variability to the usual within-partition sandwich term, so that confidence intervals account for fold-assignment uncertainty. Estimate the nuisance functions on each fold with a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected by out-of-fold loss. The estimand, orthogonal moment, and asymptotic theory remain those of the original framework.
items.sig: A persistent but under-examined vulnerability of double machine learning is that a single reported estimate inherits the idiosyncrasy of one arbitrary cross-fitting partition, injecting an uncontrolled source of Monte Carlo variability into inferences that are often treated as if exact. We address this at its root by regarding the fold assignment itself as a nuisance to be integrated out: the orthogonal-score estimate is recomputed across many independent partitions and consolidated through a median-of-means combiner whose breakdown properties confer robustness to the occasional adverse split. Just as consequentially, the inferential apparatus is brought into alignment with the procedure, decomposing the variance estimator into its within-partition sandwich component and the between-partition dispersion so that reported confidence intervals honestly reflect fold-assignment uncertainty rather than conditioning it away. Nuisance functions are learned on each fold by a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected on out-of-fold loss, and the estimand, orthogonal moment, and asymptotic guarantees are carried over intact from the established framework. The contribution speaks directly to the reproducibility concerns that increasingly govern whether such estimators can be trusted in applied causal inference.
items.pad: Viewed through the lens of disordered statistical systems, each random cross-fitting partition plays the role of a single quenched realization of disorder, and any one orthogonal-score estimate is a sample drawn at fixed disorder. Following the logic of quenched self-averaging in spin-glass theory, we suppress the sensitivity of the double machine learning estimator to the particular fold assignment by drawing many independent partitions—independent disorder realizations—and combining their point estimates with a median-of-means combiner, a robust ensemble average tolerant to heavy-tailed sample-to-sample fluctuations. We then correct the variance estimator to include the between-partition (disorder) fluctuation term on top of the usual within-partition sandwich contribution, so that the reported intervals reflect the fold-assignment disorder rather than assuming perfect self-averaging. The nuisance functions on each fold are fit with a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected by out-of-fold loss. The estimand, the Neyman-orthogonal moment, and the asymptotic theory remain exactly those of the original framework.

items.prior_work: Long-horizon multivariate forecasting has been approached through Transformer architectures with attention redesigned for efficiency: Informer (Zhou et al. 2021) introduced ProbSparse attention and a generative-style decoder to predict long sequences in one pass, followed by variants such as Autoformer and FEDformer that add series decomposition and frequency-domain attention. Zeng et al. (2023) then showed that a single linear layer applied to the raw lookback window (DLinear/NLinear), after seasonal-trend decomposition, matches or exceeds these Transformers on standard benchmarks (ETT, Electricity, Traffic, Weather). This attributed much of the Transformers' apparent gain to the experimental setup rather than to attention, and established simple linear maps as the default strong baseline.
items.base: We replace the single channel-shared linear map of the DLinear baseline with several channel-grouped linear heads. Channels are partitioned by correlation-based clustering, and each group is given its own trend and seasonal weight matrices, while the moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output are kept. The method adds a clustering step and group-specific parameters relative to the shared-weight baseline. Evaluation is on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons.
items.sig: The ongoing debate over whether simple linear predictors can rival transformer forecasters has sharpened attention on a design choice that DLinear leaves at an extreme: a single map is shared across all channels, implicitly asserting that every series obeys identical trend and seasonal dynamics. We interrogate the largely unexplored middle ground between that fully-tied model and the opposite extreme of wholly per-channel parameters, and show that correlation structure among the channels supplies a principled basis for partitioning them. Channels are grouped by correlation-based clustering, and each group receives its own trend and seasonal weight matrices, while the moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output that give the baseline its efficiency are preserved. The added machinery is deliberately spare, a clustering step and group-specific parameters, keeping the method squarely within the lightweight regime whose competitiveness is the very crux of the current controversy. We assess it on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons, so the results bear directly on how much of the linear baseline's headroom has been left unclaimed.
items.pad: We recast the shared linear forecasting map of the DLinear baseline as a problem in network community structure. Treating the empirical cross-channel correlation matrix as a weighted graph over channels, we partition the channels into communities by correlation-based clustering—modules of mutually coupled series, analogous to functional guilds in an ecological interaction network—and endow each community with its own trend and seasonal weight matrices, in place of the single channel-shared map. In condensed-matter terms this imposes a block-diagonal coupling structure on the linear operator, with intra-block weights free and inter-block weights suppressed. The moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output map are retained unchanged. Relative to the shared-weight baseline the method adds only a clustering (community-detection) step and the group-specific parameters. Evaluation is on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons.

items.prior_work: TimesFM and Chronos are transformer-based forecasters pretrained on large, heterogeneous corpora of time series. Chronos scales and quantizes real-valued observations into a fixed token vocabulary and trains a language model with a next-token objective; TimesFM uses a decoder-only patched architecture trained for direct point prediction. After pretraining, both produce forecasts for previously unseen series without task-specific training, and report competitive zero-shot accuracy against supervised baselines. Predictive uncertainty is represented through sampled trajectories or predicted quantiles.
items.base: A Chronos-style tokenized forecaster is extended to condition on exogenous covariates. Each covariate channel is scaled and quantized with the same binning scheme used for the target series, producing covariate tokens that are interleaved with target tokens along the sequence, with channel-identifying information added to the embeddings. The backbone is fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of series that carry aligned covariates, leaving the tokenizer and next-token objective unchanged. At inference, known future covariates are supplied as context and target values are decoded autoregressively as before. Evaluation compares zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets where side information is available.
items.sig: A central limitation confronting tokenized time-series foundation models is that their zero-shot fluency is purchased by ignoring the exogenous side information that practitioners in energy, retail, and operations regard as indispensable. We show that this gap can be closed without departing from the generative recipe that makes such models attractive in the first place. Exogenous covariates are folded into the very same representational space as the target: each channel is scaled and quantized under the target's binning scheme, its tokens interleaved along the sequence with the target's, and channel identity carried in the embeddings. The backbone is then fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of series bearing aligned covariates while the tokenizer and next-token objective are left untouched, a design that preserves the pretrained generative competence rather than relearning it. At inference, known future covariates enter naturally as context and target values are decoded autoregressively exactly as before. By measuring zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets where side information is available, the study isolates precisely how much predictive value these models have been leaving on the table, and charts a route toward covariate-aware forecasting foundation models.
items.pad: Borrowing the vocabulary of symbolic sequence biology, a Chronos-style forecaster already renders a real-valued series as a string over a fixed alphabet of quantization bins—much as a genome is a string over a fixed nucleotide alphabet. We extend this symbolic representation to carry parallel annotation tracks: each exogenous covariate channel is scaled and quantized with the same binning scheme used for the target series, yielding covariate letters that are interleaved with target letters along a single linear sequence, with channel-identifying marks added to the embeddings to distinguish the tracks, as one would tag co-aligned annotation lanes over a common genomic coordinate. The backbone is fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of series carrying aligned covariates, while the tokenizer (the alphabet) and the next-token transcription objective are left unchanged. At inference, known future covariates are supplied as context and target values are decoded autoregressively as before. Evaluation compares zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets where side information is available.

items.prior_work: MAML (Finn et al., 2017) meta-learns a shared parameter initialization such that a few inner-loop gradient steps on a new task yield good performance; the exact meta-gradient requires differentiating through the inner optimization, producing second-order (Hessian-vector) terms. First-order MAML (FOMAML) drops these second-order terms, approximating the inner-update Jacobian as the identity. Reptile (Nichol et al., 2018) avoids the meta-gradient entirely, running SGD on each task and moving the initialization toward the adapted parameters. Both first-order methods trade a small amount of accuracy for substantially lower compute and memory, and both anchor shared knowledge in a single point initialization.
items.base: First-order MAML and Reptile are extended with per-layer inner-loop learning rates that are meta-learned jointly with the shared initialization. During adaptation, each layer's parameters are updated using first-order task gradients scaled by that layer's learned step size, and the outer loop optimizes both the initialization and the vector of step sizes on post-adaptation task loss. The method retains the first-order compute and memory cost of FOMAML and Reptile, adding only a small set of scalar step-size parameters, and is evaluated on standard few-shot classification benchmarks against fixed-step baselines.
items.sig: First-order meta-learners trade away the second-order curvature information that lets MAML calibrate how far each parameter should move during adaptation, and they recover none of it, advancing every layer at one indiscriminate rate. We ask how much of that discarded geometry can be repurchased at first-order cost, and identify per-layer step sizes as an economical carrier of it. FOMAML and Reptile are augmented with inner-loop learning rates that are meta-learned jointly with the shared initialization: during adaptation each layer's parameters move along the first-order task gradient scaled by that layer's own learned step, and the outer loop optimizes the initialization and the vector of step sizes together on post-adaptation loss. What makes the approach compelling is that this added expressiveness is essentially free, inheriting the compute and memory footprint of FOMAML and Reptile while adding only a handful of scalar parameters, so it speaks to the accuracy-versus-cost frontier that dictates when practitioners can justifiably forgo full second-order meta-learning. We evaluate on standard few-shot classification benchmarks against fixed-step baselines to quantify exactly what this layer-wise adaptation geometry recovers.
items.pad: Framed as a question of metaplasticity—the biological regulation of how plastic each part of a system is, rather than of the plastic changes themselves—we let each layer of the network carry its own inner-loop plasticity coefficient, analogous to a layer-specific adaptive control gain or a neuromodulatory setpoint that governs the local propensity to learn. First-order MAML and Reptile are extended with these per-layer inner-loop learning rates, which are meta-learned jointly with the shared initialization. During adaptation, each layer's parameters are updated using first-order task gradients scaled by that layer's learned step size, and the outer loop optimizes both the initialization and the vector of step sizes on post-adaptation task loss—learning not only where to start but how susceptible to change each layer should be. The method retains the first-order compute and memory cost of FOMAML and Reptile, adding only a small set of scalar step-size (gain) parameters, and is evaluated on standard few-shot classification benchmarks against fixed-step baselines.

items.prior_work: Continual learning seeks to train a single model on a sequence of tasks without catastrophically forgetting earlier ones. Two dominant families address this. Regularization methods such as Elastic Weight Consolidation (Kirkpatrick et al., 2017) estimate each parameter's importance to past tasks via the diagonal of the Fisher information matrix and add a quadratic penalty that anchors important weights near their previous values. Rehearsal methods such as experience replay instead store a subset of past examples (or generate surrogates) in a memory buffer and interleave them with current-task data during training. Regularization avoids storing raw data but degrades as tasks accumulate and the penalty terms conflict; replay is typically stronger but incurs memory and privacy costs.
items.base: Combine EWC's quadratic Fisher penalty with a small experience-replay buffer in a single training objective: at each step, train on current-task data plus a minibatch sampled from the buffer while retaining the EWC anchoring term on parameters important to past tasks. The buffer supplies the replay gradient and can also be used to re-estimate the Fisher importance weights online rather than only at task boundaries. The relative weighting of the replay loss and the regularization penalty is tuned per task, and the method is evaluated on standard task-incremental benchmarks against each component in isolation.
items.sig: Continual learning has largely been organized around a dichotomy, regularization that anchors parameters against rehearsal that revisits data, yet the two act on orthogonal failure modes, and casting them as competitors obscures how naturally they compose. We unify them in a single objective: each step trains on current-task data alongside a minibatch drawn from a small experience-replay buffer, while EWC's quadratic Fisher penalty continues to anchor the parameters most consequential for past tasks. The buffer is made to serve double duty, for beyond supplying the replay gradient it lets the Fisher importance weights be re-estimated online rather than frozen at task boundaries, so the parameter-space memory tracks the model's evolving geometry instead of a stale snapshot. The balance between the replay loss and the regularization penalty is tuned per task, and we evaluate on standard task-incremental benchmarks against each component in isolation, so the design directly exposes whether parameter-space anchoring and data-space rehearsal are substitutes or, as we contend, complementary mechanisms whose union reaches further than either alone.
items.pad: Cast in the language of information geometry and statistical mechanics, EWC's quadratic Fisher penalty is a harmonic (elastic) potential that anchors the parameter particle to past-task minima along the directions of the Fisher information metric—the natural Riemannian curvature tensor on the parameter manifold—while an experience-replay buffer acts as a finite reservoir of past microstates coupled into the dynamics. We combine the two in a single training objective: at each step, train on current-task data plus a minibatch sampled from the buffer (a Monte-Carlo draw from the reservoir) while retaining the EWC anchoring term on parameters important to past tasks. The buffer supplies the replay gradient and is also used to re-estimate the Fisher metric (the local curvature of the information landscape) online, rather than only at task boundaries. The relative weighting of the replay loss and the elastic regularization penalty is tuned per task, and the method is evaluated on standard task-incremental benchmarks against each component in isolation.

items.prior_work: Kleinberg, Mullainathan, and Raghavan (2017) and Chouldechova (2017) established that several intuitive group-fairness criteria cannot be satisfied simultaneously. When base rates differ across groups and prediction is imperfect, a risk score cannot be calibrated within groups while also equalizing false-positive and false-negative rates; equivalently, predictive parity and error-rate balance are jointly unattainable. These impossibility results are derived from the observed joint distribution of scores, binary outcomes, and group membership, and hold regardless of the classifier used. They are widely cited as evidence that fairness definitions encode irreducible normative choices.
items.base: Replace the exact fairness criteria with epsilon-relaxed versions that permit bounded violations of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance. Derive the feasible region of jointly achievable (epsilon_cal, epsilon_fpr, epsilon_fnr) tuples as a function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy, extending the exact impossibility to a quantitative tradeoff surface. Estimate this Pareto frontier on standard fairness benchmarks such as COMPAS and Adult, and report how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened, providing practitioners a budget-allocation view of the incompatibility.
items.sig: The impossibility results relating within-group calibration to error-rate balance are typically read as a hard binary wall; this work recasts that wall as a measurable geometry. We replace the exact fairness criteria with epsilon-relaxed versions that permit bounded violations of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance, and derive the feasible region of jointly achievable (epsilon_cal, epsilon_fpr, epsilon_fnr) tuples as an explicit function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy, extending the exact impossibility to a quantitative tradeoff surface with genuine decision-theoretic content. Estimating this Pareto frontier on standard fairness benchmarks such as COMPAS and Adult, we report how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened, providing practitioners a budget-allocation view of the incompatibility. More broadly, the construction reframes fairness auditing from a pass/fail verdict into the study of an achievable-tradeoff manifold that downstream policy and deployment decisions can be reasoned about on.
items.pad: Borrowing the vocabulary of frustrated systems in statistical mechanics -- where mutually competing local constraints cannot be simultaneously minimized and the ground state settles at a nonzero residual frustration energy -- treat the three fairness constraints as incompatible order parameters of a frustrated Hamiltonian. Replace the exact fairness criteria with epsilon-relaxed versions that permit bounded violations of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance, reading each epsilon as the slack a constraint is granted before it breaks. Derive the feasible region of jointly achievable (epsilon_cal, epsilon_fpr, epsilon_fnr) tuples as a function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy -- a constraint-frustration phase diagram whose boundary plays the role of a coexistence surface -- extending the exact impossibility to a quantitative tradeoff surface. Estimate this Pareto frontier, i.e. the phase boundary, on standard fairness benchmarks such as COMPAS and Adult, and report how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened, providing practitioners a conserved-budget, resource-partitioning view of the incompatibility in which tightening one constraint necessarily transfers frustration into another.

items.prior_work: Deep ensembles (Lakshminarayanan et al., 2017) train several networks from independent random initializations and average their predictive distributions, yielding well-calibrated uncertainty and strong out-of-distribution detection. MC-dropout (Gal & Ghahramani, 2016) instead keeps dropout active at test time and treats multiple stochastic forward passes as approximate Bayesian posterior samples. Both estimate predictive uncertainty from the spread of a collection of predictions—across independently trained models or across sampled dropout masks. Ensembles typically calibrate better while dropout is cheaper, and the two are standard baselines for uncertainty quantification.
items.base: Combine the two estimators by keeping dropout active at test time within each member of a deep ensemble, so the final predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks. Sweep the number of members and the dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring calibration (ECE, NLL) and out-of-distribution detection against plain ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets. Report the calibration-versus-cost frontier to identify member-and-mask allocations that reach ensemble-level calibration at reduced training cost.
items.sig: Deep ensembles and MC-dropout are usually treated as competing answers to the same question; this work composes them into a single hierarchical predictive model rather than forcing a choice between them. We combine the two estimators by keeping dropout active at test time within each member of a deep ensemble, so the final predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks, capturing the between-mode diversity that ensembles supply together with the within-mode spread that dropout approximates in one estimator. Sweeping the number of members and the dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, we measure calibration (ECE, NLL) and out-of-distribution detection against plain ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets, and report the calibration-versus-cost frontier to identify member-and-mask allocations that reach ensemble-level calibration at reduced training cost. The result speaks directly to any deployment where uncertainty quality is required but full ensemble training is prohibitive.
items.pad: Cast prediction as sampling from a two-level disordered ensemble in the sense of statistical mechanics, with a quenched degree of freedom (the random initialization, frozen once each network is trained) sitting above an annealed degree of freedom (the dropout mask, resampled freely at inference, as in the quenched-versus-annealed disorder hierarchy of spin systems). Combine the two estimators by keeping dropout active at test time within each member of a deep ensemble, so the final predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks -- a partition-function-style average taken over the quenched configurations and, for each, a Monte-Carlo integration over annealed mask realizations. Sweep the number of members and the dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring calibration (ECE, NLL) and out-of-distribution detection against plain ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets. Report the calibration-versus-cost frontier to identify member-and-mask allocations -- equivalently, how to partition a fixed sampling budget between quenched and annealed disorder -- that reach ensemble-level calibration at reduced training cost.

items.prior_work: Schnabel et al. (2016) reframed recommendation as a treatment-effect problem in which observed interactions are missing-not-at-random: popular items are over-observed, so a model trained naively on observed data inherits popularity bias. Their inverse-propensity-scoring (IPS) approach reweights each observed interaction by the inverse of its propensity of being observed, yielding an unbiased estimate of the loss that would obtain under uniform exposure. In implicit-feedback settings these propensities are typically estimated from marginal item popularity, and the reweighted objective is used for both training and offline evaluation. Variance-reduction variants such as self-normalization and propensity clipping are standard companions to this estimator.
items.base: Within the inverse-propensity framework, replace the single global popularity-based propensity per item with propensities conditioned on user-side covariates such as activity level and coarse demographic group, fit with a regularized logistic model over item and user features. Combine the resulting weights with self-normalization and propensity clipping to control estimator variance, then reweight the implicit-feedback training loss as usual. Propensities are re-estimated per user segment, so highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal.
items.sig: Popularity-based inverse-propensity correction implicitly assumes every user is exposed through the same population-wide lens; this work makes the exposure model itself user-aware. Within the inverse-propensity framework, we replace the single global popularity-based propensity per item with propensities conditioned on user-side covariates such as activity level and coarse demographic group, fit with a regularized logistic model over item and user features. The resulting weights are combined with self-normalization and propensity clipping to control estimator variance, and the implicit-feedback training loss is reweighted as usual. Because propensities are re-estimated per user segment, highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal, targeting precisely the exposure heterogeneity that a single marginal cannot represent and opening a route to debiasing that accounts for who did the observing, not only what was observed.
items.pad: Cast the observed-interaction problem as an acceptance-and-efficiency correction of the kind used to unfold detector-biased event samples in experimental particle physics, where each recorded event is up-weighted by the inverse of its detection efficiency to recover the true underlying rate; here the detector is the exposure mechanism and its efficiency is the propensity of observation. Within the inverse-propensity framework, replace the single global popularity-based propensity per item -- a flat, position-independent efficiency -- with propensities conditioned on user-side covariates such as activity level and coarse demographic group, fit with a regularized logistic model over item and user features, that is, an efficiency map that varies across the phase space of users. Combine the resulting weights with self-normalization and propensity clipping to control estimator variance, the familiar stabilization of inverse-efficiency weights against small-denominator blow-up, then reweight the implicit-feedback training loss as usual. Propensities are re-estimated per user segment, so highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns -- their own local detector efficiency -- rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal.

items.prior_work: Connecting a frozen vision encoder to a frozen large language model typically relies on a trainable bridging module. BLIP-2's Q-Former (Li et al., 2023) uses a small set of learnable query tokens that cross-attend to frozen image features and emit a fixed number of visual tokens for the language model. LLaVA (Liu et al., 2023) instead applies a linear or MLP projection that maps each encoder patch feature directly into the language model's embedding space. In both approaches the image is presented to the language model as a sequence of input tokens prepended to the text.
items.base: A connector that runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel and concatenates their outputs. The Q-Former contributes a fixed set of compressed query tokens and the projection contributes the full set of per-patch tokens; a learned scalar gate weights the two streams before they are prepended to the text. The module is trained on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, so it combines the compression of query-based bridging with the spatial coverage of direct projection.
items.sig: Query-based bridging and direct patch projection sit at opposite ends of a compression-versus-coverage tradeoff in vision-language connectors; this work builds a connector that holds both ends at once. It runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel and concatenates their outputs: the Q-Former contributes a fixed set of compressed query tokens and the projection contributes the full set of per-patch tokens, with a learned scalar gate weighting the two streams before they are prepended to the text. Trained on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, the module combines the compression of query-based bridging with the spatial coverage of direct projection in a single learned interface. By letting the gate arbitrate between compact semantic summarization and dense spatial grounding, the design turns an either/or connector decision into a tunable spectrum, with implications for any frozen-backbone multimodal stack that must trade sequence length against spatial fidelity.
items.pad: Taking a cue from the two-stream organization of biological vision -- a low-bandwidth pathway carrying a compressed, gist-level summary of a scene alongside a high-bandwidth pathway that preserves fine retinotopic, spatially resolved detail -- build a connector that runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel and concatenates their outputs.
