The Q-Former contributes a fixed set of compressed query tokens (the summary channel) and the projection contributes the full set of per-patch tokens (the spatially preserved channel); a learned scalar gate, analogous to a neuromodulatory mixing weight that arbitrates between the two afferent streams, weights the two before they are prepended to the text. The module is trained on image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, so it combines the compression of query-based bridging with the spatial coverage of direct projection.

items.prior_work: DARTS (Liu et al., 2019) makes neural architecture search differentiable by relaxing the discrete choice of operation on each edge into a continuous softmax mixture within a single over-parameterized supernet, then jointly optimizing the architecture parameters (alphas) and a shared set of operation weights via bilevel gradient descent. Because every candidate subnetwork reuses the same supernet weights (weight sharing), one training run yields cheap performance estimates for the whole search space. Subsequent studies documented instability: selected cells collapse toward parameter-free operations such as skip connections, and the correlation between supernet-estimated ranking and true standalone accuracy is weak. Proposed remedies include early stopping, regularization, and perturbation-based selection.
items.base: Within the DARTS supernet, parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a systematically different scale than parameterized operations, which biases the architecture gradients toward them. The proposal normalizes each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture is formed, so that all candidates contribute at comparable magnitude, and adds a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution to slow premature collapse. The change is evaluated by the resulting improvement in correlation between supernet-derived ranking and standalone accuracy.
items.sig: The collapse of DARTS cells toward skip connections is often treated as a diffuse instability; here it is pinned to a concrete scale-mismatch mechanism and addressed at its root. Within the DARTS supernet, parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a systematically different scale than parameterized operations, which biases the architecture gradients toward them. The proposal normalizes each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture is formed, so that all candidates contribute at comparable magnitude, and adds a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution to slow premature collapse. The change is evaluated by the resulting improvement in correlation between supernet-derived ranking and standalone accuracy, the very quantity whose weakness undermines weight-sharing search, making the intervention a direct attack on the estimator-fidelity bottleneck at the center of differentiable architecture search.
items.pad: Read the supernet's edge as a competitive neural circuit in which candidate operations vie for representation, and recall that -- as in cortical circuits stripped of gain control -- units with systematically larger output amplitude come to dominate regardless of intrinsic usefulness. Within the DARTS supernet, parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a systematically different scale than parameterized operations, which biases the architecture gradients toward them. Borrowing the homeostatic, divisive-normalization principle by which biological neurons rescale their responses onto a common dynamic range, the proposal normalizes each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture is formed, so that all candidates contribute at comparable magnitude, and adds a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution -- a thermodynamic temperature term that keeps the competition hot and postpones symmetry-breaking -- to slow premature collapse. The change is evaluated by the resulting improvement in correlation between supernet-derived ranking and standalone accuracy.

items.prior_work: Post-training quantization (PTQ) compresses large language models to low bit-widths without retraining. GPTQ (Frantar et al., 2023) quantizes weights layer-by-layer using approximate second-order (Hessian) information from a small calibration set, greedily rounding columns and updating the remaining weights to compensate for the induced error, enabling accurate 3-4 bit weight quantization. Complementary work on outlier-aware quantization (LLM.int8, Dettmers et al., 2022) observes that a small number of feature dimensions carry disproportionately large activation magnitudes that dominate quantization error, and isolates these outlier dimensions into a higher-precision path while quantizing the rest. Together these establish the standard PTQ recipe: Hessian-guided error compensation for weights plus explicit protection of a fixed set of outlier channels.
items.base: Extend GPTQ's Hessian-guided quantization by combining it with the outlier-channel protection from LLM.int8: identify the highest-magnitude activation channels from calibration statistics, keep the corresponding weight columns in higher precision, and run GPTQ's second-order error compensation over the remaining 4-bit columns. A per-layer threshold on calibration activation magnitude selects which channels to protect, and the protected fraction is tuned to trade off model size against accuracy. This yields a mixed-precision GPTQ variant where the error-compensation update accounts for the already-fixed high-precision columns.
items.sig: Two of the most consequential threads in post-training quantization—Hessian-guided error compensation and outlier-channel protection—have matured along separate tracks, leaving unresolved how second-order weight updates ought to behave when a subset of columns is deliberately withheld at high precision. We resolve this coupling within a single objective. Calibration activation statistics identify the highest-magnitude channels, whose weight columns are retained in higher precision, while GPTQ's second-order error compensation is run over the remaining 4-bit columns with explicit accounting for the already-fixed high-precision set. A per-layer threshold on calibration activation magnitude governs channel selection, and the protected fraction becomes a principled dial for navigating the model-size against accuracy frontier. The outcome is a mixed-precision GPTQ formulation in which error compensation and outlier protection reinforce one another rather than operate in isolation—a coupling with direct bearing on deploying accurate 4-bit language models at scale.
items.pad: Cast low-bit weight compression as a lossy source-coding problem in the Shannon rate-distortion sense: the calibration set is an empirical source, the bit-width is the coding rate, and the layer's second-order curvature supplies the distortion metric on weight space (a Fisher-information-like geometry). As in strongly-correlated physical systems, the activation statistics are heavy-tailed, with a sparse subset of feature coordinates concentrating most of the spectral mass. The scheme pins these dominant coordinates, i.e. the highest-magnitude activation channels identified from calibration statistics, to a high-precision reservoir, holding their weight columns out of the aggressive 4-bit regime, and then relaxes the remaining columns toward a constrained minimum-distortion configuration using second-order (Hessian-guided) error compensation that is conditioned on the already-pinned high-precision columns. A per-layer threshold on calibration activation magnitude acts as the order parameter deciding which coordinates enter the reservoir, and the pinned fraction is swept to trace the resulting rate-distortion (size-versus-accuracy) frontier. Mechanically this is GPTQ's greedy second-order error compensation operating over a fixed high-precision channel subset in the manner of LLM.int8, re-expressed in the vocabulary of rate-distortion coding and constrained energy minimization.

items.prior_work: The Lottery Ticket Hypothesis (Frankle & Carbin 2019) showed that dense networks contain sparse subnetworks that, trained from the original initialization, match full-network accuracy; iterative magnitude pruning with weight rewinding is the standard method for finding them, removing the smallest-magnitude weights. Movement pruning (Sanh et al. 2020) instead scores weights by how far they move away from zero during fine-tuning, outperforming magnitude pruning in transfer settings. Both are unstructured methods operating on individual weights. Structured pruning removes whole units — channels, filters, or attention heads — yielding direct hardware speedups but typically lower accuracy at matched sparsity because of its coarser granularity.
items.base: At a fixed parameter budget, rank structural groups (convolutional channels and attention heads) by an importance score aggregated from movement-pruning statistics — the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow in magnitude during fine-tuning — and remove the lowest-scoring groups. Compare this against the same structured procedure using aggregated weight magnitude (group L2 norm) as the score. Both variants use iterative pruning with weight rewinding to the original initialization. Evaluate at matched sparsity levels, reporting task accuracy and measured inference latency across vision and language backbones.
items.sig: Movement-based importance has reshaped how the field scores individual weights, yet its logic has stayed confined to unstructured pruning—precisely the regime whose sparsity confers no direct hardware benefit. We ask what that signal delivers once it is lifted to the level of structural groups that hardware can actually exploit. At a fixed parameter budget, convolutional channels and attention heads are ranked by an importance score aggregated from movement-pruning statistics—the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow in magnitude during fine-tuning—and the lowest-scoring groups are removed. This is placed head-to-head against the identical structured procedure driven instead by aggregated weight magnitude (group L2 norm), with both variants using iterative pruning and weight rewinding to the original initialization. Evaluating at matched sparsity while reporting task accuracy alongside measured inference latency across both vision and language backbones isolates whether a movement signal's advantage survives the coarser granularity that gives structured pruning its practical value—a determination that sits directly on the path to deployable sparse models.
items.pad: Borrow the framing of activity-dependent synaptic pruning in developing nervous systems, where whole functional units are eliminated according to a use-it-or-lose-it selection signal rather than by the strength of individual connections. Treat each structural group — a convolutional channel or an attention head — as such a functional unit, a circuit module competing for survival at a fixed parameter budget, and assign it a fitness score by aggregating movement-pruning statistics over its constituent weights, namely the summed tendency of those weights to drift away from zero (to grow) during fine-tuning, read as a developmental trajectory of reinforcement. The lowest-fitness modules are culled. As a control that swaps the activity-dependent selection signal for a purely static one, the identical group-level culling is instead driven by aggregated weight magnitude (the group's L2 norm). Both regimes use iterative elimination with rewinding of the survivors to their original initialization, echoing a critical-period reset. At matched sparsity (matched culled fraction) the survivors are evaluated for task accuracy and measured inference latency across vision and language backbones.

items.prior_work: LoRA (Hu et al., 2022) adapts a pretrained model by freezing its weights and adding a trainable low-rank update BA to selected weight matrices, so each layer's effective weight becomes W + (alpha/r) BA. The rank r and scaling factor alpha are hyperparameters; in standard usage a single rank is applied uniformly across all adapted layers and chosen by manual sweeping or heuristic defaults. This keeps the number of trainable parameters small relative to full fine-tuning while recovering much of its performance. Rank and scaling are typically fixed before training and held constant throughout.
items.base: Under a fixed adapter-parameter budget, assign each LoRA layer a rank in proportion to a cheap statistic of its pretrained weight matrix (for example, its stable rank or spectral energy) computed once before training, instead of applying a single uniform rank to all adapted layers. Ranks are rounded to meet the budget, while the scaling factor and the rest of the LoRA parameterization are unchanged. The resulting allocation is compared against uniform-rank baselines at matched parameter counts.
items.sig: Uniform rank assignment is the near-universal default in low-rank adaptation, yet it tacitly treats every layer as equally deserving of capacity—an assumption at odds with the well-documented heterogeneity of pretrained weight spectra. We recast rank selection as a budget-allocation problem and let the model's own structure decide it. Under a fixed adapter-parameter budget, each LoRA layer receives a rank in proportion to a cheap statistic of its pretrained weight matrix—its stable rank or spectral energy—computed once before training, with ranks rounded to meet the budget and the scaling factor and the remainder of the LoRA parameterization left untouched. Measured against uniform-rank baselines at matched parameter counts, this allocation probes a broader principle with wide reach for parameter-efficient fine-tuning: that where capacity is placed, not merely how much is spent, is a decisive and largely untapped lever.
items.pad: View the fixed adapter-parameter budget as a total channel capacity to be distributed across a bank of parallel subchannels, exactly the allocation problem solved by water-filling in communication theory, where more capacity is poured into the subchannels that carry more usable signal energy. Here each adapted layer is a subchannel, and its usable energy is read off once, before training, from a cheap spectral statistic of its frozen pretrained weight matrix — its stable rank or spectral energy, interpretable as an effective dimensionality or participation ratio in the sense of localization physics. Each layer's LoRA rank is then allocated in proportion to that statistic rather than held uniform across layers, with ranks rounded to respect the total budget, while the scaling factor and the remainder of the low-rank parameterization are left untouched. This energy-proportional (water-filling-style) allocation is benchmarked against uniform-rank baselines at matched total parameter counts.

items.prior_work: Loss-correction methods address training with noisy labels by estimating a class-conditional noise transition matrix T, where T_{ij} is the probability that a true label i is observed as label j. Patrini et al. (2017) use this matrix for forward correction (composing the network's predictions with T before the loss) or backward correction (multiplying the per-class loss vector by T^{-1}), yielding an objective that is unbiased for the clean-label risk under class-conditional noise. The transition matrix is typically estimated once, from a warm-up model's predictions on high-confidence anchor examples. This line of work is motivated by Zhang et al. (2017), who showed that deep networks can memorize arbitrary noisy labels, making explicit noise modeling important for robustness.
items.base: Extend forward loss correction by re-estimating the class-conditional noise transition matrix at fixed intervals during training rather than once from a warm-up model. At each re-estimation step, high-confidence anchor examples per class are identified from the current model, and the transition matrix is updated as an exponential moving average of the successive estimates. The corrected loss is then computed with this running matrix. The approach keeps the class-conditional noise assumption and the forward-correction objective, changing only the schedule of transition-matrix estimation and its stabilization across updates.
items.sig: A quiet assumption underpins loss-correction methods for label noise: that a transition matrix estimated once from a warm-up model remains an adequate description of the corruption process for the entire span of training. But the very estimator—the network's high-confidence predictions—sharpens as training proceeds, rendering a one-shot matrix an increasingly stale target. We treat the transition matrix as a quantity to be tracked rather than frozen. Forward loss correction is extended so the class-conditional matrix is re-estimated at fixed intervals during training; at each step, high-confidence anchor examples per class are drawn from the current model, and the matrix is updated as an exponential moving average of the successive estimates, with the corrected loss computed against this running matrix. The class-conditional noise assumption and the forward-correction objective are preserved intact—the sole change is the estimation schedule and its stabilization across updates—sharpening the method into a question of how noise models should co-evolve with the networks they correct.
items.pad: Model the corrupted labels as the output of a discrete memoryless channel whose confusion (transition) matrix maps a transmitted class i to a received class j — precisely the class-conditional noise matrix of loss correction. Rather than estimating this channel once during a warm-up and assuming it is time-invariant, treat it as a slowly drifting channel and track it online with a decision-directed scheme borrowed from adaptive equalization: at fixed intervals the model's own high-confidence per-class decisions serve as pilot (anchor) symbols from which a fresh transition-matrix estimate is formed, and the running channel estimate is updated as an exponential moving average of the successive estimates, i.e. a first-order recursive filter with a forgetting factor. The forward-corrected loss — the network's predictions composed with the transition matrix before the loss — is then evaluated with this tracked matrix. The class-conditional channel model and the forward-correction objective are unchanged; only the estimation is made recursive and stabilized across updates.

items.prior_work: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection commonly relies on post-hoc scores computed from a trained classifier's outputs. Hendrycks and Gimpel (2017) introduced the maximum softmax probability (MSP) baseline, in which in-distribution inputs tend to receive higher maximum class probabilities than OOD inputs. Liu et al. (2020) proposed the energy score, defined as the negative temperature-scaled log-sum-exp of the logits, which aligns more closely with the input's density under the model and empirically improves over MSP. Both scores are computed from a single forward pass of a fixed, pre-trained network without additional data.
items.base: The energy score is computed as a single scalar from the logits and compared against a global threshold. Because different classes exhibit systematically different energy magnitudes, the method estimates, for each class, the mean and standard deviation of the energy over in-distribution training inputs assigned to that class. At test time, an input's energy is standardized using the statistics of its predicted class, and the standardized value is thresholded for OOD detection. The correction requires only stored per-class statistics and no retraining or auxiliary outlier data.
items.sig: Energy-based out-of-distribution detection rests on a single global threshold applied to a scalar derived from the logits—a design that tacitly presumes energy magnitudes are comparable across classes. They are not: in-distribution energies vary systematically from one class to the next, so a global cut necessarily over-flags some classes while under-flagging others. We remove this confound with a lightweight per-class calibration. For each class, the mean and standard deviation of the energy are estimated over in-distribution training inputs assigned to that class; at test time, an input's energy is standardized using the statistics of its predicted class, and the standardized value is thresholded for detection. Requiring only stored per-class statistics—no retraining, no auxiliary outlier data—the correction repositions a widely used score onto a common scale, with implications for any deployment where energy-based detection must hold across a heterogeneous label space.
items.pad: Treat the scalar energy read from the logits as a measured observable subject to a class-dependent systematic offset, the way a metrologist treats a gauge whose zero-point shifts between instrument settings, or an astronomer who must standardize raw luminosities into comparable standard candles before they can be placed on a common scale. Because each class exhibits a systematically different characteristic energy magnitude, the method calibrates a per-class reference distribution from the in-distribution training inputs assigned to that class, storing that class's mean and standard deviation of the energy. At test time the observable is converted to a z-score against the reference of its predicted class — subtract that class's mean, divide by its standard deviation — and an out-of-distribution decision is made by comparing the standardized value to a single control limit, in the spirit of a statistical-process-control chart. Only the stored per-class summary statistics are required; there is no retraining and no auxiliary outlier data.
